text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with a PMHx of Crohn's disease on Remicade every 6 weeks, prednisone, and recently started on ___, and asthma presenting with c/o fever and URI symptoms, with cough, improved with steroids and antibiotic therapy. ACUTE ISSUES: ================== # Sepsis due to URI: Patient met multiple SIRS criteria on admission, unclear source at this time although given cough/congestion suspect viral versus bacterial URI, with sinus tenderness, ear fullness, and clear CXR, however lower resp infection also on ddx given immunosuppressed state. Given immunosuppressed status and recent hospitalization initially covered broadly for HCAP/CAP with vanc/cefepime/azithro, as well as tamsulosin for possible influenza, and supported with IVF. She was also started on stress dose steroids with hydrocortisone given chronic steroid use (for Crohns Disease, see below). Patient's tachycardia/hypotension has responded to IVF as well as stress steroids. By HD2, symptoms had largely resolved. Micro data ultimately growing aspergillus from sputum, negative rapid screen for influenza. ID was consulted, and felt aspergillus was a contaminant, and felt comfortable sending home to complete short 5 day course of antibiotics, so was transitioned to monotherapy with PO azithromycin (vanc/cefepime/tamsulosin stopped) and patient was discharged with one further dose to complete the 5 day course. Her steroids were successfully tapered back to her home dose of 6cc prednisolone (15mg/5cc solution) by the time of her discharge, and her pressures remained stable. # Hypotension: Initially presented with hypotension to SBP ___ the ___, likely due to an infection, though given chronic steroid use can't rule out element of adrenal insufficiency contributing. Resolved with stress dose steroids (hydrocortisone) and fluids, as well as treatment on antibiotics for presumed respiratory source. Ultimately tapered back to her home dose of prednisolone, and her blood pressures continued stable. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================== # Asthma: Patient with intermittent wheezing on exam on admission, did not appear ___ distress and not hypoxic, breathing comfortably on RA throughout her hospitalization. Following treatment of presumed respiratory infection as well as albuterol treatments, her wheezing resolved. # Crohn's: Presentation not consistent with an acute flare of her Crohns Disease. ___ discussion with her outpatient GI physician, her ___ was stopped given concern for active infection. She was given stress dose steroids with hydrocortisone as discussed above, and ultimately tapered back down to her home dose of prednisolone. She was instructed to continue her home dose of 6cc prednisolone (15mg/5cc solution) without tapering, and to continued off the ___ until her next outpatient appointment with Dr. ___. She was continued on PCP prophylaxis with ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== #Patient will take one more dose of azithromycin 500mg on ___ ___ was discontinued this admission per outpatient GI physician ___ remained off medication on discharge; will remain off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ to determine when to restart. #Will continue 18mg prednisolone without further tapering while off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ #Emergency Contact: ___ Relationship: Husband Phone number: ___ #Code status: FULL
summary: You came ___ with a fever and cough, with sinus pressure and congestion, ___ the setting of chronic immunosuppressive therapy for your Crohns disease. You were treated with some fluids and antibiotics, and we got an x-ray of your chest to look for deeper lung infection which was clear. Your symptoms improved with the fluids and antibiotics, and we transitioned you over to oral antibiotics. The infectious disesaes team came to see you while you were here because your sputum culture showed a fungus called aspergillus, however they agreed that this was only colonization ___ the respiratory tract, and not an active infectious process causing your symptoms.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with PMHx of fibromyalgia, chronic pain syndrome, myofascial pain syndrome, peripheral nueropathy, hypertension and anxiety who presents with diffuse body pain over the past three months in the setting of discontinuation of her prior narcotic medications for her pain syndromes. # Back Pain: Ms. ___ presented to ___ with diffuse body pains over the past three months in the setting of her previous prescribing not being able to prescribe narcotics. On presentation she did mention that she had back pain which caused her to fall and led to urinary incontinence. On further questioning it was determined that the urinary incontinence was not true urinary incontinence, but was rather an inability to get to the bathroom in time to urinate. Neurology was consulted in the Emergency Department and did not believe there was a neurologic cause of her symptoms, as there were no neurologic deficits noted on examination. She had good strength and sensation in her extremities and DTRs were normal. To rule out an epidural abscess an MRI was obtained which did not show any evidence of cord compression. Ortho-spine saw patient and did not believe any intervention was required. Etiology was likely flare in her fibromyalgia/myofascial/chronic pain syndromes. On physical examination, she did not display pain when distracted. To control her pain she was continued on her home dose of her baclofen, gabapentin, and lamotrigine. During her hospitalization she also received oxycodone for pain relief. The oxycodone was discontinued at the time of discharge. At the time of discharge she was ambulating with a walker without difficulty. Physical therapy was consulted but given the fact that she was walking to and from her bathroom without a walker, a physical therapy evaluation was not necessary. She deferred home physical therapy. Occupational therapy was also consulted. They recommended home safety evaluation and home occupational therapy upon discharge. # Fibromyalgia/Myofascial Pain Syndrome: Her diffuse body pain over the past three months is likely related to her fibromyalgia/myofascial/chronic pain syndrome. Continued with pain management with her home medications of baclofen, gabapentin, lamotrigine. Oxycodone was given during hospitalization, although she does not have a prescription of this at home. Thus, oxycodone was discontinued at the time of discharge. Ms. ___ able to ambulate with a walker without pain at the time of discharge. # Anxiety: Ms. ___ has history of anxiety. She was previously on clonazepam but this prescription expired prior to coming to the hospital. Her anxiety remained stable during hospitalization without the use of clonazepam. We recommended she contact Psychiatry services at ___ at ___ for further evaluation and management. # Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease: Ms. ___ has a history of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. She was continued on her daily dose of omeprazole 20 mg PO daily. # NEUROPATHY: Ms. ___ has a history of peripheral neuropathy. We continued her home medications including gabapentin and lamotrigine. # ASTHMA: Ms. ___ has a history of asthma. She did not have wheezing during hospitalization but did have albuterol prn for wheezing/sob. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== # PSYCHIATRY: Ms. ___ sought Psychiatry referral service. We provided the number to ___ Psychiatry at ___ with her discharge paperwork. # CONTACT: ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ with worsening diffuse body pain over the past three months. This was likely in the setting of worsening of your fibromyalgia/chronic pain syndrome/myofascial pain. Since you noticed back pain, we obtained an image of your spine (Magnetic Resonance Imaging-MRI). The results of this image was reassuring with no evidence of any infection present and no compression of your spinal cord. You were treated with your home medications (baclofen, gabapentin, lamotrigine) as well as a short dose of oxycodone for your pain. With the improvement of your pain, your strength increased and you were able to walk the halls with a walker. We attempted to get a physical therapy evaluation but because you were able to walk on your own, no physical therapy evaluation was needed.

summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath, wheezing and cough that we think were most likely caused by bronchitis, possibly from a viral infection. You improved with conservative treatment including nebulizers, cough medicines, and a short course of steroids. Your blood sugars were elevated because of the steroids, but they will go back to normal once you complete the course of steroids (prednisone). We also found that you have evidence of increased fluid in your lungs and ankles from your heart failure, so we re-started a medication you had been on previously (called Torsemide) at a lower dose than you had previously been on, and we are stopping your hydrochlorothiazide. You should weigh yourself every day at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT/PLAN: ___ yo male history of hyperlipidemia, gastritis, hypertension, anxiety, alcohol abuse, presenting with acute shortness of breath with hypoxemia evidence on ABG now improved on supplemental O2 and heparin gtt. #Hypoxia: Patient presented with new hypoxia with repeat V/Q scan showing v/q mismatch in RLL c/f PE. He underwent non-con CT which is not protocoled for PE, but given the findings, radiology was highly suspicious for PE. Heme/onc was consulted as pt reported that he had genetic susceptibility testing the reports that he had abnormal metabolism of warfarin. Per heme/onc he was started on lovenox with anti-Xa levels to be monitored as an outpatient. He also had expiratory wheezing which improved with nebs c/f obstructive airway disease. He underwent PFTs which showed obstructive pattern that is likely more chronic that patient realized per Pulm. He was discharged on albuterol and advair with pulmonary follow-up as an outpatient. #Troponin Leak: Likely cardiac strain. His troponins have trended down even with his CKD. His PE was managed as above. He will follow-up with pulmonary and cardiology as an outpatient. #CKD: Chronic stage III. US of kidneys not suggestive of obstruction or parenchymal disease. Recent UA bland. SPEP/UPEP were unremarkable. All meds were renally dosed and creatinine was monitored daily. #Alcohol Abuse: Reportedly drinks heavily per family members. No history of acute withdrawal or seizures, although did get defensive when discussing alcohol use. His recent epidoses of diaphoresis, tachypnea and hypertension may be related to alcohol withdrawal given that his last drink was now 48-72 hours ago. He was started on a CIWA and treated with diazepam for withdrawal symptoms. After 72 hours he was transitioned back to his home clonazepam. SW consultation was offered given the patient's need to be on systemic AC for PE as above, but patient declined. He prefers to speak with his personal acquantainces regarding alcohol cessation. #HTN: continued chlorthalidone, atenolol was changed to metoprolol given CKD. His reactive airway disease remained stable on Beta blockade while in the hospital. #GERD: continue omeprazole #Anxiety: continued home citalopram and clonazepam after cessation of CIWA scale as outlined above # Code: Full (discussed with patient) Wife ___ ___ Son ___ MD ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient should f/u with heme/onc and pulm as outpt for further management of PE and Obstructive lung disease. - Patient should be sure to have age-appropriate cancer screening, as unclear etiology for his PE
summary: you were admitted to ___ with complaints of chest pain. You were found to have a blood clot in one of the arteries in your lungs and will need to be on blood thinners for at least 3 months. You were also diagnosed with obstructive lung disease (asthma) and will be discharged on two new inhalers. It is imperative that you not drink alcohol while on blood thinners as falls can increase your risk of stroke or life threatening bleeding. Additionally, you mentioned you take Klonipin/aka Clonazepam for anxiety. These medications can make you sedated and greatly increase your risk of falls and should be used with extreme caution. Please note, we changed your medication ATENOLOL to METOPROLOL as METOPROLOL is better metabolized in patients with ___ diseas rather than ATENOLOL.

She was Observed for stress test, but was wheezy and unable to do stress on ___ so was admitted for inpt stress. # Chest pain - Given pt's history, it seems most likely to be MSK chest pain precipitated by cough, and she was breathing shallowly in order to instigate cycle. ___ also represent angina, although pt is very healthy at baseline and does not have exertional angina making unstable angina less likely. No tachycardia, hypoxia, pleuritic CP to suggest PE. No known CHF or symptoms or exam findings to suggest CHF exacerbation. She was given nebulizers with improvement in wheezing. text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a hx of bipolar d/o, s/p pacer placement, osteoarthritis, and hypertension, who presents to the ED with bright red blood per rectum and lightheadedness. Developed non-radiating left sided chest pain and was transferred to ___ for evaluation for possible acute coronary syndrome. . # CAD: The patient had no known history of CAD. She developed acute onset of chest pain that awoke her from sleep and lasted an unclear amount of time. She was admitted to the cardiology service to rule out an MI. Her cardiac enzymes were cycled and were negative x3. Her last exercise stress test was negative. She had a stress MIBI on ___ that revealed that there was a fixed defect at the apex. There was no evidence of acute ischemia based on this study. Her lipid panel was obtained and was as follows: ___ 129 Chol 227 HDL 92 LDL 109. She was continued on simvastatin 10mg daily. She was started on a low dose beta-blocker given the results of her stress test. . # AV block s/p permanent pacemaker: A-sensed V-paced rhythm. No need to interrogate device at this time. Has a ___ Adapta dual-chamber pacemaker. She was continued on telemetry. Her device was not interrogated during her admission because her dizziness resolved. . # Bloody stool: She had a CT scan on admission that revealed no signs of colitis on CT scan. Her hematocrit remained stable ranging from 36.4 to 39 during her hospital stay. She remained hemodynamically stable. She had an EGD with no signs of gastritis or ulcers. She had a colonoscopy that was aborted due to incomplete bowel prep. She had a repeat colonoscopy that revealed a colonic polyp and diverticulosis. The polyp was removed and was sent to pathology. . # Hypertension: Baseline SBP 170-190s. Her SBP remained in the 130-150s. She was continued on amlodipine 5mg daily, enalapril 20mg daily and HCTZ 25mg daily . # Endometrial thickening: CT scan revealed moderate amount of soft tissue density/fluid in endometrial cavity and radiology recommended obtaining a pelvic U/S to r/o endometrial neoplasm. Pelvic U/S revealed that she had normal endometrial lining (~4mm), but pt. deferred a vaginal ultrasound. . # Asthma: stable. She was continued on her home albuterol and advair prn shortness of breath/wheezing. . PROPHYLAXIS: -Pain management with percocet and apap -Bowel regimen with senna and colace . summary: You were admitted after you developed black tarry stool and dizziness. In addition, you also experienced chest pain prior to admission. You were transferred to the cardiology floor to rule out a possible heart attack. During your hospital stay, you had a stress test that did not show any acute problems with your heart but did show an old scar showing some mild previous damage. After the stress test, you had an endoscopy and colonoscopy to search for sources of bleeding. The study revealed that you had a polyp. The polyp was removed and sent to pathology. You were found to have hemorrhoids. Please refrain from self-disimpaction to prevent bleeding from your hemorrhoids. We have started you on colace to help soften your stool.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo F with DMII, HTN, stage IV breast cancer dx ___ s/p neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Adriamycin, Cytoxan and Taxol) followed by surgery, radiation, and ongoing treatment for recurrent disease to the lung since last year who presented with cough, hypoxemia, and dyspnea with unrevealing chest imaging suggestive of viral URI, with CT demonstrating metastases and concern for mass effect at T8. summary: As you know, you were admitted here for your coughing and wheezing symptoms. A chest xray did not show evidence of pneumonia. Your symptoms were most likely due to the spread of breast cancer to the lungs. We started you on an antibiotic for a possible infection, as well as steroids, an albuterol nebulizer, and atrovent to relieve your wheezing. Cat scan Imaging of your chest showed a mass on your ___ that was concerning for compression of your spinal cord. We obtained additional imaging, an MRI which again showed the mass on your spinal cord. The radiation oncologists were consulted and felt that the pain on your left side that you have been having is most likely due to this mass on your spinal cord. Therefore you started radiation treatment to your ___ to help relieve your pain and shrink this mass. You also received a unit of blood to help with your weakness, as you were found to be anemic when you arrived.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with PMHx of asthma s/p intubation in ___ who p/w SOB c/w asthma flare and allergic reaction. . # Shortness of breath: Symptoms were secondary to asthma exacerbation as well as an allergic reaction/angioedema. No clear exposure history although reportedly did have swelling of face and lips and does have some swelling under left eye. Patient has history of similar reaction to ibuprofen, but he does not recall taking anything similar. Patient was treated with epipen at OSH and responded well. On neck x-ray, there was some concern for soft tissue swelling, but CT neck with contrast ruled this out. Given some wheezing on exam and improvement with nebs, also component of asthma exacerbation. Patient was started on prednisone and treated with albuterol and ipratropium nebs. He will complete as prednisone taper on discharge. Patient reported some symptoms slightly suspicious for pleuritic chest pain. D dimer was elevated at 788, but patient was not tachycardic. This pain improved with prednisone and nebs, and his oxygen saturation levels improved; thus, suspicion for pulmonary embolism was very low and CTA was deferred. On discharge, patient will complete the prednisone taper and continue asthma medications. . # GERD: Continued home prilosec. . TRANSITIONS OF CARE: -full code -should f/u with scheduled ENT appointment at ___ for evaluation of known polyps -will complete prednisone taper (see discharge meds) -Recommend outpatient Pulmonary follow-up
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with difficulty breathing secondary to asthma exacerbation as well as an allergic reaction. You were treated with epinephrine for the allergic reaction. There was some concern initially for swelling in your throat, but a CAT scan showed that you did NOT have this, which was reassuring. It is unclear what your allergy was to, but since you had similar symptoms in the past from taking ibuprofen, you should avoid ibuprofen containing medices such as motrin and advil. For the asthma, we treated you with prednisone and nebulizers. You responded well and did not require any oxygen. On discharge, you should complete a prednisone taper (slowly decrease the dose) as per instructions below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ female with a medical history of well-controlled childhood asthma who presented with symptoms consistent with asthma exacerbation, transferred for MICU for respiratory distress requiring intubation. Hospital course complicated by exertional tachycardia and pulmonary embolism. # Respiratory Distress / Wheezing / Cough: On admission, patient with significant audible wheezing with poor air movement bilaterally. Despite treatment with continuous nebulizer treatments and high-dose intravenous steroids, the patient developed tachypnea to ___ with rising lactate. The patient was transferred to the MICU, where the decision was made to intubate. Once intubated, patient's ventilatory mechanics were not felt to be consistent with high airway resistances. She was extubated after one day. Upon extubation, she was noted to have centralized monophonic wheeze, raising suspicion for vocal cord dysfunction. ENT was consulted; Bedside nasopharyngeal fiberoptic exam demonstrated good motion of her cords bilaterally, and no evidence of PVFM (Paradoxical Vocal Fold Motion). Wheezing was appreciated when vocal cords were open per ENT report. Anti-anxiolytics had no effect on her symptoms. Pulmonary recommended CT airway to further evaluate; this showed limited study due to active coughing but significant caliber change and collapse of the mid to distal trachea that was strongly suggestive of tracheobronchomalacia. It was felt that the patient should be re-evaluated by both ENT and pulmonary as outpatient to re-evaluate VC motion and re-peat CT airway. The patient was discharged on prednisone taper, low-dose Advair, and ipratropium nebulizer as needed. Working diagnosis at the time of discharge was cough-variant asthma, pending further evaluation by pulmonary and ENT. # Segmental PE: CTA on ___ demonstrated segmental pulmonary embolism with extension of thrombus into the subsegmental vessels in the right lower lobe. CTA was conducted given continued tachycardia with ambulation. She was started on lovenox with a plan to follow-up with her PCP as an outpatient. # Tachycardia: Primarily with ambulation. Though to be secondary to deconditioning and pulmonary embolism. Improved by the time of discharge. Transitional Issues: - Re-peat CT Airway to be conducted by Pulmonary team as outpatient. - Re-evaluation of VC motion by ENT. - Duration of anti-coagulation to be determined by patient's PCP.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and wheezing. While we evaluated many possibilities to explain your breathing difficulty, we do not have a clear explanation at this point. You may have cough-variant asthma, a vocal cord that is not functioning normally, or perhaps a collapsible windpipe. You will continue to investigate these possibilities further with your outpatient doctors. ___, your breathing improved over the course of your hospital stay. You were also noted to have a blood clot in your lung during your hospital stay, for which you will be treated with a blood thinner.

summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart attack and required a procedure (heart catheterization and angiography) to evaluate both the chambers of the heart and the blood vessels surrounding the heart. The pressures within your heart were not grossly abnormal and there were no major blood vessel blockages. You also had an ultrasound of your heart, which revealed that the pumping function of your heart had recovered, back to normal. This recovery is most likely the result of fast reperfusion (blood flow/delivery) of the heart tissue after your heart attack. The electrophysiologists came to examine you and decided that you need an implantable defibrillator, which was placed before discharge. You will need to continue taking oral antibiotics for 2 more days in order to prevent infection. Please weigh yourself at the same day everyday. If your weight is more than 165lbs you should take 40mg furosemide once daily until your weight falls.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE: ___ yo male with HIV (CD4 678) presented with dyspnea at both rest/exertion and hypoxia to low ___ on room air for several days. ACUTE ISSUES ============ #Hypoxia, dyspnea on exertion, and wheezing: Etiology unclear, but attributed to an unspecified reactive airway disease, perhaps triggered by a viral illness. Chest CT showed no interstitial disease, but other relatively non-specific findings. PFTs showed no gross abnormalities, however, interpretation was limited as patient received bronchodilator 1 hour prior to test. On day of discharge he had no oxygen requirement, however, he was discharged with supplemental oxygen and albuterol amps with nebulizer in case he has further episodes of hypoxia. He will follow-up with pulmin ___ weeks and get a TTE with bubble study and repeat PFTs as an outpatient. #Hyponatremia: Sodium 130 on admission. Most likely hypovolemic in the setting of dyspnea and increased insensible losses vs SIADH release due to acute lung process. No changes in mental status. Resolved with PO intake. His long-acting glargine was increased while inpatient, but he was discharged back on his home regimen, as he had finished his prednisone. He was instructed to call his PCP if BG>300 at home. #Anemia, macrocytic: Patient is near baseline (___). #HIV: Last CD4 count 678 in ___. On atripla at home. summary: You were admitted due to low levels of oxygen in your blood. Chest xray did not show any explanation. You were empirically started on breathing treatments, steroids and antibiotics for a possible exacerbation of reactive airway disease. We consulted the pulmonary specialists, as this is not the first time that you have presented with low oxygen levels. They recommended several studies to evaluate your lungs. Chest CT did not show signs of interstitial lung disease, and the other findings were relatively non-specific. You also had pulmonary function tests, which were relatively normal, but the interpretation was limited because you had a breathing treatment prior to the test. The pulmonary doctors would ___ to see you in their clinic to further evaluate you in ___ weeks. They recommened an ultrasound of your heart, which was not done during your stay, and a repeat lung function test before seeing them. You can schedule these appointments by calling the numbers below. You will be sent home with supplemental oxygen and breathing treatments to use with nebulizer machine for shortness of breath/wheezing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old with hep C cirrhosis, alcoholism, admitted with likely acute on chronic hepatitis and confusion. . # Hematemesis: small amount on ___, with NG lavage that did not ___, was transfered to the MICU for emergent EGD and observation. EGD revealed two grade 2 varices and one grade 1 varix with no evidence of bleeding as well as portal gastropathy. Given no evidence of variceal bleed, the patient was transferred back to the floor. The patient then underwent EGD for variceal banding ___ and tolerated the procedure well. He will follow up with Dr. ___ need repeat EGD 3 weeks after discharge. Given his hematemesis and guaiac positive stool, he was maintained on ciprofloxacin and iv ppi bid that was then trasnitioned to PO. He received one unit of pRBCs ___ for acute blood loss anemia. . # Confusion: Mr. ___ confusion was attributed to his acute hepatitis on chronic liver disease and he was treated with lactulose and rifaximin. Infectious work-up including diagnositic paracentesis to r/o SBP was negative. B12 levels were normal. His confusion improved prior to discharge. . # Ascites/cirrhosis/Acute Hepatitis: SAAG on diagnostic tap consistent with portal hypertension due to liver cirrhosis. Acute hepatitis was evaluated with liver ultrasound which showed no clot or gallbladder pathology on U/S. Acute hepatitis was felt to be most likely due to the patient's alcohol abuse as hepatitis C viral load was not very high. When the patient's liver tests worsened on ___ he was started on predinsone for acute alcoholic hepatitis. This was discontinued after his hematemesis. Hepatology was consulted and followed the patient closely on this admission. He was briefly started on nadalol but developed wheezing, so that was discontinued. The patient was discharged on sprinolactone and lasix. . # Acute Renal Failure: Baseline 0.9 from ___. It was felt the patient was intravascularly dry and this was the cause of his renal failure, not hepatorenal syndrome. The patients diuretics were stopped, he was given a unit of pRBCs, intravenous fluids and albumin with improvement in creatinine. . # Anemia : HCT was 29.5 in ___, down from 40 in ___. Pt had guaiac + stool and episode of hematemesis as noted above. After his banding procedure serial HCTs were stable prior to discharge. He will recheck his HCT on ___ and have the results faxed to Dr. ___. . # Coagulopathy: attributed to liver dysfunction and poor nutrition. . # ETOH: active drinker. The patient and his family were informed that he is not a liver transplant candidate due to his alcoholism and that he must stop drinking completely due to his liver disease. The patient was given thiamine, folate and a multivitamin. Social worker saw the patient during this admission. . # Hypertension: Patient normotensive on floor. He was discharged on sprinolactone and lasix per above. . # Asthma/? COPD: Patient was given albuterol PRN SOB/wheeze . # Tobacco Abuse: patient was encouraged to stop smoking and provided a nicotine patch while in the hospital. . The patient was maintained on a low sodium diet, a bowel regimen with lactulose, and no heparin sc given his INR was greater than 2. The patient was FULL CODE during this admission. Communication was with the patient and his daughters, ___ and ___ ___
summary: You were admitted for confusion which was due to worsening of your liver disease. You were started on rifaximin and lactulose to help with your confusion. You need to continue with these medications. You were found to have a irritation of your stomach lining due to your liver disease and drinking. You are bleeding from your stomach currently. You have been started on protonix and sulcrafate to help protect your stomach and decrease the bleeding. Please have your blood checked on ___. These results should be faxed to Dr. ___. You were also found to have kidney damage which improved with blood tranfusions and fluids. You were found to have large blood vessels in your esophagus (throat) from your liver disease. These blood vessels can bleed and lead to your death. You had a banding procedure on ___ ___. You will need to have one more banding procedure.

summary: You were admitted for treatment of cellulitis. You were also found to have acute kidney injury, probably from the infection in your leg. You were given IV antibiotics and your infection started to improve, and you were switched to oral antibiotics. You received fluids to help your kidney function resolve. You had labs drawn prior to leaving the hospital and you will be contacted with the results. In the meantime, you should continue to drink lots of water and fluids to continue to help your kidneys. Please see the attached sheet for your updated medications. START Bactrim DS 1 tab twice a day for 10 days START Augmentin 875mg twice a day for 10 days Please continue to take these antibiotics until they are finished even if all of your symptoms resolve.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female history of CAD (medically managed), HTN, TIA, recurrent diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, CKD (baseline ___, diet-controlled DM2, who presents with several days of episodic dyspnea and productive cough. #Dyspnea, productive cough: Ms ___ presented with 4 days of fatigue, paroxysms of productive cough, dyspnea, and wheezing. She had chest and abdominal soreness related to coughing; no fevers, or hemoptysis. She was well appearing, without tachycardia, fever or hypoxemia, and her exam was only positive for expiratory wheezing (patient not on long acting beta 2 agonist or steroids at home). Labs were notable for: lack of leukocytosis, and elevated D-dimer; flu swab negative. CXR without evidence of pneumonia or pulmonary edema and LENIs were negative. Her constellation of symptoms was most consistent with a viral respiratory infection, likely bronchitis given lack of nasal/sinus symptoms. Antibiotics were not given. She was given symptomatic treatment with benzonatate, dextromethorphan-guaifenesin, Tylenol, and ipratropium/albuterol nebs PRN. Because her asthma symptoms suggested she had moderate persistent asthma, she was given a prescription for Advair HFA and she will follow up with her primary care physician. #Elevated D-dimer: Patient's D-dimer elevated at 3124 on presentation. Given her complaint of dyspnea and chest pain, this was initially concerning for possible PE. However, symptoms seem infectious in nature and ___, EKG and CXR are negative. She has no risk factors like recent surgery or bedrest, no prior DVT/PE, no hemoptysis, and although she has a history of recurrent lymphoma she is not currently under treatment for an active malignancy. She remained afebrile, heart rate was normal and she was not hypoxemic on room air at rest or on ambulation. Further, there are alternative causes to explain her elevated D-dimer like infection (although she lacks SIRS criteria), chronic renal failure and underlying cardiovascular disease. Because of this, she was not started on empiric anticoagulation and V/Q scan (bc she would not have CTA in setting of CKD) was deferred. #Hypertension BP elevated on admission but improved during the day. She was continued on her home regimen: -metoprolol succinate 75mg PO daily -HCTZ 12.5mg PO daily -losartan 50mg PO daily #CAD She was continued on home aspirin, metoprolol, and statin. #History of gastritis: We reduced her home dose of ranitidine to 150mg PO daily from BID given CKD. #Chronic pain The patient has chronic pain due to arthritis. Her pain is mostly in her lower back and in her knees. She is on the narcotics contract for Percocet, which she takes up to three times daily. However, she only necessitated Tylenol PRN to control her pain during hospitalization. #CKD: Patient with hx of CKD, likely due to HTN and DM. BUN/Cr monitored, and nephrotoxic agents were avoided/medications were renally dosed TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [ ] She was started on Advair given her moderate persistent asthma symptoms. She can discuss this with her primary care provider on follow up appointment on ___. [ ] Ranitidine was decreased from BID to daily due to CKD. [ ] Blood cultures collected in ED: 1 aerobic bottle grew GPCs in clusters. Most likely contaminant as patient has no leukocytosis, remained afebrile, and had no other concerning symptoms. Primary team will follow up final results. Patient was informed of the result and consulted that should she become febrile, have rigors, chills or aches, or any other concerning symptom, she should return to the ED or call primary care provider.
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of shortness of breath, productive cough and an abnormal lab. After imaging, and monitoring you overnight, it is believed that your shortness of breath, and your cough are due to a viral respiratory illness that inflamed your lungs giving you "bronchitis." This most likely worsened your underlying asthma also and is why you have been needing your inhalers more frequently. A chest X Ray was done, which did not show any evidence of pneumonia. You also had ultrasounds done of your legs, which did not show any clots. Because you had been using your asthma inhaler frequently, we are discharging you with a daily inhaler to use to prevent asthma symptoms. You can discuss continuing this medication with Dr. ___ at your next appointment, which is on ___ at 1:40pm (see below for details). There was also one blood culture bottle out of four that was positive for bacteria. We think that this finding is likely a contaminant from your skin. You do not have a fever, or elevated white blood cell count, and do not think you have this bacteria in your blood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ==================== Pt is a ___ yo F w/ ___ notable for asthma requiring intubation as a child who presented with 1 week of fever, chills, generalized myalgias, cough and SOB, found to have asthma exacerbation and lobar PNA, treated with 5 day course of steroids and levofloxacin. Hospital course complicated by repeated episodes of SVT (believed to be orthodromic AVRT). TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== [] Will need closer follow up and mgmt plan for asthma, discharged on fluticasone BID, can likely consider just doing PRN when having URIs in the future [] Patient had several episodes of SVTs, can reconsider electrophysiology study and ablation if she continues to have recurrent episodes of palpitations or syncope once recovered from pneumonia [] Recommend discontinuing Adderall given SVTs [] treponemal Ab for Syphilis still pending at discharge [] incidental finding of 2mm LUL nodule on CT chest ___, unlikely to require interval imaging given low risk. ACUTE ISSUES: ============== # R middle lobe pneumonia Pt presented with one week of fevers, cough, myalgias, initially thought to be c/w viral URI. CXR on admission was not concerning for PNA. However, pt's sx of cough worsened and she became febrile with a productive cough and ongoing wheezing, and repeat CXR (___) showed e/o right middle lobe pneumonia. Flu swap, resp panel/cx neg, negative urine legionella/step pneumo, blood cxs. She was treated with a 5 day course of levofloxacicn. Lack of improvement by D3 (___) so got CTA which was unremarkable and troponins neg. Symptomatic cough relief by the time of discharge. # Asthma Exacerbations Pt has hx of severe asthma exacerbations requiring intubation as a child, in the absence of URIs actually has intermittent asthma. On admission treated for asthma exacerbation iso lobar PNA. Pt was given prednisone 50 mg (5 day total treatment). Levalbuterol and ipratropium nebs used instead of albuterol due to SVTs as below. Discharged with ipratropium as well as fluticasone which she can continue to take until PCP follow up, at which point it may be appropriate to just take fluticasone during periods of URIs. She notes a history of heart racing episodes with albuterol, of note. # SVT On presentation, pt had asymptomatic SVT episodes to 170s thought to be due to albuterol. She then triggered on the floor with HRs 200s, initial EKG showed a narrow complex tachycardia with P waves consistent with either atrial tachycardia given different morphology P waves from baseline EKG vs retrograde P waves but with prolonged RP interval which would be consistent with orthodromic AVRT. Per EP suspect this was likely a reentrant rhythm with long RP. Rhythm recurred during hospitalization, sometimes responsive to vagal maneuvers. Unremarkable TTE. Plan to f/u with EP if this continues after treatment of her PNA. # Constipation Pt with constipation, abdominal discomfort, and bloating, resolved with bowel regimen. # Bacterial vaginosis Pt complained of sx of vaginal discharge while inpt, given 1 day fluconazole. She was screened for GC/CT/HIV which were negative but treponemal Ab for Syphilis was still pending at discharge. # Uptrending BUN, resolved Pt's BUN uptrending during admission, likely ___ poor PO intake and dehydration, resolved. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # ADD Pt is on Adderall at home which she will frequently take when she is working. Consider stopping as outpatient iso newly found SVT given stimulant effect # Anxiety Pt rarely takes Xanax 1 mg for anxiety. Given Ramelteon for insomnia while inpatient.
summary: You had fevers, chills, feeling fatigued, and shortness of breath concerning for an asthma exacerbation and were found to have pneumonia in your right lung You were given steroids for the asthma exacerbation and the initial thought was this was a virus. You ultimately developed a pneumonia which we saw on a chest X-ray, so you were given antibiotics (levofloxacin) for 5 days. You continued to have a cough so we gave you medications to help with your symptoms and pain. You had episodes of your heart going very fast so were seen by the Cardiologists. This was most likely due to frequent use of the inhalers in addition to your infection and being on steroids. Thir heart rhythm is not dangerous but if it continues to bother you, your primary care doctor ___ schedule you to see the cardiologists as outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a pleasant ___ female with severe asthma, diabetes on oral hypoglycemics, hypertension, osteoarthritis, illiteracy, who was sent to the ___ by her PCP by phone evaluation for continued and worsened shortness of breath and wheezing. She complained also of a mildly productive cough with white sputum, had poor air movement and was treated for asthma exacerbation. #. Asthma Exacerbation: Patient has history of frequent ___ visits and hospitalizations for asthma exacerbation, particularly in the ___ and ___ time. She reports no changes in home or work environment, no smoke exposure, and no changes in detergents or soaps at home that could cause allergen induced exacerbation. This exacerbation was likely triggered by a viral upper respiratory infection, as she was having upper respiratory symptoms. She was treated with azithromycin for possible pneumonia by the ___ the weekend prior to admission, but her chest Xray in the ___ ___ showed no signs of pneumonia. She was not started on any antibiotics during this hospitalization. The patient was treated with combivent nebulizers in the ___ then started on Q4 hours standing albuterol nebulizers on the floor overnight. She was also started on 60mg of prednisone for 5 days, to be tapered down as an outpatient. She was continued on her home medications of Advair and montelukast. A sputum culture could not be sent due to poor quality of specimen. She was ruled out for flu with negative DFA from nasopharyngeal swab and low suspicion. The following description represents the course of her steroid taper: Prednisone 10 mg Tablets as directed: ___ 6 tablets until ___ (total of 60mg) 5 tablets on ___, 4 tablets on ___, 3 tablets on ___, 2 tablets on ___, 1 tablet on ___, then off. In the ___, the patient's peak flow improved from 150 to 180 after nebulizer treatment. Her peak flow on discharge was 200, and she was ambulating with O2 saturation of 95% without shortness of breath. She was encouraged to follow her peak flow at home and seek medical attention more quickly if it starts to drop. She will continue home nebulizer treatments as needed but should return if she is requiring nebulizers more often than every four to six hours. She was set up with a visiting nurse to help monitor her improvement and be compliant with treatments. Because she is illiterate, the patient may need repeated asthma education in the outpatient setting to help reinforce an Asthma Action plan specific to her needs. It may help to label her home peak flow meter with red, yellow, and green stickers to remind her when to start using her PRN medications and when to get help. . #. Diabetes Mellitus Type 2: Patient's home regimen includes glyburide and metformin; she states that her sugars typically run in the 170s at home. Her last HbA1c in ___ was 6.9. Her metformin was held initially, so in the setting of prednisone therapy for the asthma exacerbation, her blood sugars were significantly elevated into the 300s on the first day of hospitalization. Upon restarting the metformin, her blood sugars were still in the 200s. She was sent home on an insulin sliding scale to be used for blood sugars >200 just during the brief course of prednisone therapy with the help of the ___. Ms. ___ was set up with a visiting nurse to help with insulin teaching and administration and to ensure compliance with fingersticks. The patient has had to self-administer insulin in the past during courses of steroid therapy; she also regularly checks her blood sugars at home ___ times daily. . #. Hypertension: Her blood pressure was controlled on her home regimen of valsartan and HCTZ. . #. Vaginal Yeast Infection: Patient has recurrent vaginal yeast infections. She was given pads to keep her underwear dry after urination. She was continued on clotrimazole cream applied daily to vaginal area. . . Ms. ___ was kept on a diabetic diet during this hospitalization. She was given subcutaneous heparin for DVT prophylaxis.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having another asthma exacerbation. You were started on prednisone therapy to help you decrease the inflammation in your lungs. You should continue your nebulizers at home as needed to help improve your breathing. Please be sure to check your peak flow every day to make sure it is not getting worse (just prior to leaving the hospital it was 195). After starting the prednisone, your blood sugars were more elevated than usual and harder to control. Please continue to check your blood sugars at home every day ___ times per day. The home visiting nurse ___ help give you some insulin if your blood sugars are very high. Continue to use your nebulizer treatment up to 4 times a day as needed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ F with hx of asthma (no formal PFTs, on albuterol inhaler only) who presented to ___ with cough and wheezing, consistent with asthma exacerbation. # Asthma # Viral infection Pt presented to the ED after several visits with her PCP for cough/wheezing/URI sx at the end of ___. Her breathing improved with standing duonebs, IV methylprednisolone, and aggressive cough suppressant medications: standing Tylenol, guaifenesin with dextromethorphan q3H, standing codeine q4h, Tessalon perles. Albuterol Inhaler 1 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN Shortness of breath 2. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID
summary: You came to the hospital because you had a severe cough and wheezing. While you were in the hospital, you were given nebulizers, IV steroids, and aggressive cough medications. Your cough improved on this regimen. Since you had already finished a 5-day antibiotic course (Azithromycin) before you came to the hospital, you were not given any more antibiotics. You had both xray and CT imaging that showed no infection or other problem in your lungs. When you leave the hospital, you will likely have a persistent cough for a week or so due to the inflammation in your airways, but it should improve with time, as it has in the hospital. You will continue on the cough medications, flovent inhaler twice per day, and prednisone. You will gradually decrease the prednisone dose (see below).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o female with PMHx of afib & seizures here with confusion in the setting of fentanyl patch and Klebsiella UTI. . # Confusion: The most likely etiology was infection vs medication effect. Pt was found to have a urinary tract infection, initially treated with Ceftriaxone and transitioned to po Ciprofloxacin to complete a 10 day course. The confusion appeared to coordinated with the increase in fentanyl which was stopped on admission. ___ has been well controlled with standing tylenol and lidocaine patch. Pt had a CT chest that showed uncomplicated pleural effusion without obvious signs of infection or mass. After discussion with family, decision was made not to pursue further diagnostic work up for effusions. Pt showed no signs of agitation and mental status improved in hospital, it will likely continue to improve on return home and after correction of sleep wake cycles. . # A.Fib: Pt had a few episodes of AFib with rapid ventricular rate thought possibly related to infection vs mild dehydration. It is also possible that pt has poorly controlled rates at home. Pt was given some gentle fluids and started on Metoprolol 25mg TID with better rate control. Pt was continued on home regimen of Diltiazem ER 360mg & 120mg qhs. Coumadin was decreased to 3mg qhs and pt was discharged with a therapeutic INR of 2.2. She will need continued INR monitoring at rehab as the level is likely to fluctuate due to concommitant treatment with Ciprofloxacin. . # ___ edema: Pt with chronic venous stasis changes & superficial skin breakdown that improved in hospital with elevation & local wound care. Pt can taking Lasix 20mg daily prn lower extremity swelling. Pt will need ongoing teaching regarding lower extremity elevation & local wound care. . # HTN: No acute issues in house. Pt was continued on Diltiazem home regimen & Metoprolol 25mg TID was added for better rate control. . # Asthma: Pt was noted to have diffuse wheezes on the morning of ___ & ___ that resolved completely with Albuterol Nebulizer treatments. CXR showed no evidence of infiltrate and oxygen saturations remained in mid ___ on room air through these episode. Pt should continue with Albuterol nebullizer treatments & Cromolyn inhaler as needed for wheezing . # Osteoarthritis: The fentanyl was stopped on admission due to change in MS. ___ well controlled with tylenol ___ QID & lidocaine patch. . # Code: DNR/DNI confirmed with patient & family.
summary: You were admitted with confusion and found to have a urinary tract infection. We have been treating you with Ciprofloxacin & we have discussed this with your neurologist. Dr. ___ it is safe continue the Ciprofloxacin with the Keppra without any additional risk of seizure. You developped a rapid heart rate with underlying atrial fibrillation intermittently and we have started a new medication called Metoprolol 25mg three times daily to help control this rapid rate. You should continue taking Ciprofloxacin 500mg twice daily for another 9 days. We have decreased your Coumadin to 3mg daily and you will need regular INR monitoring at rehab. We have stopped the Fentanyl & we have been treating your ___ with a Lidocaine patch & Tylenol. We have started daily Calcium and Vitamin D replacements to help strength your bones.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with cardiomyopathy, last EF 45%, p/w progressive SOB, DOE, and CP in the setting of stopping her spironolactone though without pulm edema on CXR and not appearing significantly volume overloaded. . # CHF: The ___ had dyspnea on exertion in the setting of stopping her spironolactone with evidence of failure on exam. An echo was performed which showed her EF to be ___. Her failure was thought to be secondary to her atrial fibrillation with RVR. She was diuresed with lasix throughout her hospital stay and her symptoms improved with diuresis and rate control of her atrial fibrillation. . # Atrial fibrillation: The ___ did not initially respond to metoprolol, therefore she underwent digitalis loading as her pressures were borderline throughout her episodes of RVR. While undergoing digitalis loading her rate was controlled and dropped from the 130s down to 70-80s. She was also continued on metoprolol during this time, and was discharged on 100mg metoprolol succinate. She was continued on coumadin . #. Chest Pain: She has a remote normal cath from ___ and a normal stress EKG from ___. She was ruled out with 3 sets of normal cardiac enzymes. . #Asthma - daily advair was started to treat asthma and reduce nighttime wheezing. . #. HTN: Enalapril and carvedilol were held as metoprolol was uptitrated to achieve optimal rate control. She was restarted on enalapril. . #. Hypothyroidism: The ___ was continued on levothyroxine. Her thyroid function tests were normal, ruling this out as a cause of her tachycardia. . #. Hyperlipidemia: The ___ was continued on lipitor.
summary: You presented to the hospital with shortness of breath. It was found that you had a fast heart rate related to your atrial fibrillation that likely caused this symptom. You were started on a new medicine called digoxin to help control your atrial fibrillation. In addition, your Coreg was changed to metoprolol to help control your atrial fibrillation. You are also being discharged on a medicine called lasix to help keep fluid out of your legs and lungs. You will need to have your blood electrolytes checked regularly while on this medicine. No other changes were made to your medical regimen. You will need to continue to take coumadin an have your INR checked regularly because of your atrial fibrillation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of asthma and anemia, who presented with one week of increasing cough, shortness of breath, and wheezing, and was admitted for management of an asthma attack. CXR negative for acute pulmonary process. She was treated with Albuterol and Ipratroprium nebulizers, with significant improvement, and will continue albuterol at home. She was given a prednisone 40 mg PO daily (d1 = ___, d7 = ___, and will been seen in follow up for further management if necessary. Symptomatically, the patient has mild persistent asthma in cooler months and moderate persistent asthma in the warmer months. Thus, she was initiated on fluticasone 110 mcg 2 puffs BID. She was also given a peak flow meter and an asthma action plan to further guide her care. # Asthma Exacerbation: ___ y.o F with hx of asthma, presenting with asthma exacerbation, likely exacerbated by URI symptoms x 1 week. CXR negative. Based on her symptoms, the patient has mild persistent asthma in the cooler months (symptoms 2 or more times per week, nighttime awakening ___ times/month, and Albuterol use greater than 2 times/week). She has moderate to severe persistent asthma in the warmer months (daily symptoms, nighttime awakenings daily, Albuterol use 3 times/day, and severe interference with daily activity). She was treated with duonebs and initiated on prednisone with a plan for a 7-day burst. She was initiated on inhaled fluticasone and given education about an asthma action plan to further guide her care. # Anemia: Admission Hb of 11.8, stable from a baseline of ~10. Normocytic. Iron studies unremarkable, although patient takes iron at home. Peripheral smear when reviewed by the medicine team significant for microspherocytes and teardrop cells, with previously Hb electrophoresis significant for Hb A2. ___ represent B thalassemia trait. Remained stable during this admission. Transitional Issues: - Patient will need to make an appointment at ___ ___ (___) for follow up for the week of ___. - Continue prednisone 40 mg daily (d1 = ___, d7 = ___, to be seen in follow up to determine if further taper necessary - Initiated on inhaled fluticasone 110mcg 2 puffs bid this admission, will need to taper down if symptoms are controlled - Peripheral smear by medicine team review showed microspherocytes, tear drop cells, target cells, and previously Hb electrophoresis was significant for Hb A2 (not quantified due to normal MCV), taken together this is concerning for B thalassemia trait/minor. Iron studies normal (pt currently on prenatal vitamin with iron) further workup deferred to PCP. Anemia very mild this admission at baseline, with normal MCV.
summary: You came in with one week of increasing cough, shortness of breath, and wheezing, and were admitted for management of an asthma attack. We treated you with nebulizers for your breathing. We also put you on an oral steroid called Prednisone. Your last dose will be on ___. We prescribed you a new inhaled steroid called Fluticasone. This will help with long-term control of your asthma. You should take 2 puffs in the morning and 2 puffs at night every day even if you do not have symptoms (see your attached asthma action plan) We also prescribed you a peak flow meter to monitor your asthma. You should use this once per day, and during attacks. You can use your Asthma Action Plan worksheet to guide you in which inhalers to use.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient presented to the ED on ___ with tachycardia and SOB. In the ED, she was given albuterol/ipratropium nebs, prednisone 60mg, and guaifenesin to treat a suspected acute asthma exacerbation. IVF were given to treat tachycardia which was likely from dehydration + large doses of albuterol with resolution of tachycardia. Peak flows were tested and found to be 150 prior to treatment and 190 afterwards. No baseline PFTs were available for comparison. On admission to the floor, an ECG was taken to rule out cardiac causes of the patient's symptoms and a CXR was taken to evaluate the focal wheezing in the RLL heard on exam. While non-suggestive of an acute pulmonary process, the CXR did show significant herniation of the stomach into the chest cavity which is a very interesting finding especially givent he fact that the patient has adult onset asthma (in last few years) and GERD with onset a few years ago. Although it was believed that pt ___ had an exacerbation from her allergies, we strongly recommend a barium swallow and/or endoscopy to determine the full extent of the hernia as well as a outpatient evalutation by surgery since this hernia may be responsible for the heartburn as well as worsening (or even causing) airway hyperreactivity. In addition, if this testing indicates the stomach is at risk for becoming incarcerated, surgical intervention is necessary by elective repair. Pt did very well and was even on RA once arriving to the floor. She was inctructed to start taking flovent twice daily until told to stop by an MD, to take 40mg of prednisone for 3 days, then stop, as part of a steroid burst, to only take albuterol as needed as a rescue medication and not standing (as she ahd been doing for a while), and to start taking omeprazole twice daily as microaspirations of acidic fluid as happens with GERD likely is exacerbating airway hyperreactivity. We also strongly recommended that she should have pulmonary function tests done after recovering from the acute exacerbation to document asthma and her baseline.
summary: You were admitted and treated for an asthma exacerbation. You should have pulmonary function tests done after recovering from the acute exacerbation. You likely had an exacerbation from your allergies although we also discovered that you have a gastroesophageal hernia. We strongly recommend a barium swallow and/or endoscopy to determine the full extent of the hernia as well as a outpatient evalutation by surgery since this hernia may be responsible for your heartburn as well as worsening (or even causing) your asthma. In addition, if this testing indicates the stomach is at risk for becoming incarcerated, surgical intervention is necessary by elective repair.

summary: You were admitted for worsening shortness of breath and for a second opinion regarding your underlying lung disease. You were seen by our pulmonologists (lung doctors) who felt that your worsening shortness of breath was due to an asthma exacerbation. We increased your prednisone to 40mg daily for this exacerbation. We would like you take prednisone 40mg daily for a total of five days (day 1 = ___, day 5 = ___, then go back to your home prednisone dose of 20mg daily. We started you on a new medication called atovaquone to help prevent infections that can occur in patients taking long term steroids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ with history of seizure/pseudoseizure d/o, spina bifida, asthma/COPD p/w seizures and wheezing/shortness of breath. . # SOB/cough/wheeze: Her presentation was consistent with a COPD exacerbation. She was started on prednisone and azithromycin and was treated with a total of a 5 day course. She was also placed on standing albuterol and ipatropium nebs. Her advair was held while she was on the oral steroids. . # Seizures - She has h/o seizures, pseudoseizures, and or conversion disorder. Her phenytoin level was subtherapetutic at admission likely related to having missed doses of her medication. She received loading dose of dilatin in the ED. She had several episodes of possible pseudoseizure in her first twelve hours of hospitalization. She received Ativan for one of these episodes. She had no additional seizures after this. She was continued on her home dosing of dilantin. . #Urinary Tract Infection: She was found to have a UTI from a sample taken from her ostomy bag. She was treated with a 7 day course of bactrim. . # HTN: She was continued on her HCTZ and ACEI while in the hospital. Her b-blocker was held during part of her hospitalization due to low blood pressure. She was discharged on her home b-blocker. . # Depression: She was continued on citalopram and seroquel. . # GERD: She was continued on omeprazole. . # Home safety: Social work was consulted and ___ was set up to help with medication administration. A social worker ___ will also go to her house. The patient was provided with the proper ostomy supplies as her ostomy was leaking at home. . # Communication: With patient and spoke with friend ___ ___ ___ ___ on Admission: Gen: middle age woman, slow speech. HEENT: Muddy sclera. MMM. Neck: No JVD, no LAD, supple. Cor: RRR no m/r/g Pulm: CTAB, no audible wheezes. Abd: urostomy on RLQ. BS present. Abd soft, non-tender. Extrem: b/l ___ atrophy. Skin: + tinea on the soles of feet bilaterally. Ulcer on the sole of the left foot at the foot pad. Neuro: Oriented to person, "hospital" only. Awake and slow speech. Cranial nerves intact. Upper extremity tremor. Paraplegia, ___ strength b/l ___. GU: large uterine prolapse
summary: You were admitted with a COPD exacerbation and with possible seizures. For your COPD you were treated with azithromycin and prednisone. Your treatment is complete. It is important you continue your home inhalers. You were found to have a urinary tract infection and you were started on bactrim. It is important you finish this course of antibiotics. Your phenytoin level was low which could of caused you to have a seizure. We are not sure if you actually had a seizure in the hospital. It is important to take your phenytoin as directed to prevent seizures. You were given supplies for your ostomy bag.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y.o. man with history of asthma and tobacco abuse presenting with exacerbation of his chronic cough and increasing dyspnea consistent with an asthma vs COPD exacerbation. 1) Asthma exacerbation: The patient presented with exacerbation of his chronic cough as well as dyspnea likely due to asthma exacerbation. Given his benign chest radiograph, wheezing, and eosinophilia asthma exacerbation was considered the most likely diagnosis. He briefly desaturated with a paroxysm of coughing in the ED but then recovered and remained without further hypoxia at rest. He was treated with prednisone burst, standing bronchodilators, montleukast, and corticosteroid inhaler with good effect on his dyspnea and cough. Attempts were made to better manage possible element of COPD and his allergic rhinitis (see below) and he was discharged on standing ipratroprium/albuterol inhaler for five days, to complete a five day course of prednisone, and with instructions to continue fluticasone inhaler, montleukast, and his home Levalbuterol PRN until follow up with pulmonary. He will follow up with pulmonary as an outpatient to work on better asthma control and also for assessment of PFTs to help detect any element of coexistent COPD and manage that as well. 2) ?COPD: The patient has a long smoking history and though his history of childhood asthma and eosinophilia suggest this presentation more likely due to an asthma flare he may have coexistent COPD. Therefore, when he complained of increased sputum production on hospital day 3 he was started on a five day course of azithromycin as if for a chronic bronchitis flare. He also was discharged on anticholinergic as well as beta agonist inhaler therapy. Otherwise treatment of COPD and Asthma flares are essentially identical. He will follow up with pulmonary for better evaluation of the reversiblity of his reactive airway disease. 3) Allergic Rhinitis: The patient has chronic postnasal drip that likely is contributing to cough and he reports exposure to allergens seemed to have been part of his worsening. He was on azelastine nasal spray, which was continued. He was also started on fluticasone nasal spray and cetirizine with some effect though it was much to early to see full nasal steroid benefit at time of discharge. He will follow up with his ENT physician and allergist. 4) Tobacco Abuse: The patient was cautioned multiple times on the dangers of cigarette smoking as a trigger for his asthma as well as potentially contributing to more permanent damage to his lungs. He expressed an intent to quit. 5) Cough syncope: During a severe paroxysm of coughing the patient was noted to have an episode of syncope in the presence of the medical attending. Regained consciousness in <5 seconds spontaneously and was completely stable, not confused, and without any deficits. He reports this has happened approximately two times before. Patient was strongly cautioned on being aware of this and making sure to sit down or avoid dangerous situations until cough is better controlled. He knows he should sit down or pull over his car should he have severe coughing fits. 6) His hypertension was stable on his home amlodipine and metoprolol 7) GERD: He was continued on home ranitidine and omeprazole. He was discharged to follow up with his PCP and pulmonary clinic. Transitional Issues: -Leukocytosis: The patient had a leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance without bands at the time of discharge. This was thought most likely due to steroids. He will have this rechecked at PCP follow up.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ because you have had much worse coughing recently, with some shortness of breath. Your cough has been occurring for months. We believe that this is due to an exacerbation of asthma, most likely complicated by allergic factors and possibly underlying lung disease. Congratulations on stopping smoking. If you were to continue to smoke, it is likely that you would have more frequent lung issues, and any underlying lung disease would only get worse. While in the hospital, you received oral steroids (Prednisone), which should help to bring down the inflammation in your lungs. You should take the prednisone (40mg by mouth) through ___. While in the hospital, we took you on a walk test down the ___, during which you breathed normally. We have also started you on Singulair, Flonase and a fluticasone inhaler, all of which should improve your breathing and potentially improve your cough. We also believe that you should start on Zyrtec as well. For your home albuterol inhaler, you should use a spacer, which improves the delivery to your lungs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with hx asthma, bipolar disorder, PCOS, IBS, Hep C transferred from OSH for management of left maxillary abscess, strep viridans bacteremia, and RLQ abdominal pain. Active Issues #Left maxillary abscess: OMFS was consulted and per their evaluation there was no drainable maxillary abscess. They recommended continued IV antibiotic management as well as outpatient follow-up for extraction of several teeth. # Strep viridins bacteremia: Likely secondary to maxillary abscess. Cultures from ___ were positive from ___ and ___ growing Strep viridans. Strep viridans from ___ sensitive to ceftriaxone and vancomycin. Started on ceftriaxone ___, this was changed here to vancomycin per ID recs on ___ as ceftriaxone not well studied in strep viridans strains with high PCN MICs. She has remained afebrile since transfer, and surveillance blood cultures have remained negative. TEE performed on ___ showed no evidence of valvular vegetations or abscesses. Despite clear evidence of endocarditis on ___, ID recommended treating with vancomycin for a total of 4 weeks as strep viridans is very high risk for seeding heart valves. Pt. was discharged with PICC line to complete 4-week course of vanc. Current dosing based on initial vanc trough is 1500mg BID. She will require weekly labs including vancomycin troughs, electrolytes, CBC, and LFTs. # Medication allergies - Pt. has had allergic reactions to many different antibiotics in the past. She has been pretreated with Benadryl in the past, prior to ABx administration. On ___, pt. had a reaction to a dose of Flagyl. This reaction consisted of intense pruritis as well as significant wheezing. She was treated with IV Benadryl, solumedrol, famotidine, and albuterol nebs. Her symptoms resolved within 5 minutes. Although she does not have a specific history of vancomycin allergy; the decisino was made to premedicate each dose of vancomycin with 50mg PO benadryl. Pt. tolerated this premedication well. # RUE thrombophlebitis RUE venous u/s showed no evidence of DVT on RUE u/s. Resolving. # RLQ abdominal pain: CT scan at OSH showed possible terminal ilieitis. MRI pelvis showed no evidence of septic arthritis or osteomyelitis. Pt with hx of IBS. Throughout hospitalization patient complained of severe RLQ pain. Exam fluctuated during admission. Occasionally patient had significant rebound tenderness on exam, other times there was only tenderness to palpation over RLQ. She initially received percocet for pain, but stated that this had little effect on her pain. Pain regimen was increased to Dilaudid. As pain seemed to be improving, pt was transitioned back to Percocet ___ on ___. Pt. has had similar episodes of RLQ pain worked-up with several CTs and colonoscopy in ___, all of which were unrevealing. Pt. did not have BM for 5 days despite escalating bowel regimen. GI was consulted and recommended MR enterography to evaluate for potential enteritis (inflammatory vs. infectious). She will follow-up with GI as an outpatient for colonoscopy. Inactive Issues # Asthma: - continued home Advair, albuterol - PRN duonebs for shortness of breath # Bipolar disorder: - continued home Zoloft, lorazepam # Hepatitis C: untreated Transitional Issues: Pt. will need weekly lab draws for vancomycin trough, electrolytes, LFTs, and CBC, while on vancomycin. Will need to follow-up on final read of MR enterography.
summary: You were transfered here from ___ for a tooth infection, bloodstream infection, and abdominal pain. You were seen by the oral surgeons, who have recommended having several teeth extracted as an outpatient at ___. Your blood infection, which was likely caused by your dental abscess, is being treated with the IV antibiotic vancomycin. Your abdominal pain has been evaluated by our GI doctors and they have recommended a colonoscopy as an outpatient to determine the cause of your pain. For now, we plan to treat your pain with Tylenol. We want to avoid opioid pain medications because these will likely make your constipation and abdominal pain worse. The results of your abdominal MRI will be reviewed in detail at your gastroenterology appointment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ woman with h/o asthma, sarcoidosis, LUL lung resection and recurrent pneumonias who presented with pleuritic chest pain, fevers, and CXR and CT findings concerning for pneumonia. # Chest pain: Patient presented with severe left lateral and right anterior pleuritic chest pain. CXR and CTA chest revealed a focal consolidation in the left lower lobe involving the pleura, the differential for which included an early vs. resolving pneumonia and malignancy. There was no radiographic evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection. Cardiac enzymes and EKG were normal. Given concern for pneumonia, she was started on a 5 day course of levofloxacin. Patient remained afebrile and had no leukocytosis. She was never hypoxic and ambulatory saturations were normal. Patient's left-sided pain significantly improved, but her right-sided pain persisted (though was reasonably controlled on Tylenol, ibuprofen, and po Dilaudid). Patient was discharged with PCP follow up and was scheduled an appointment in Rheumatology to evaluate other possible causes of pleuritis (DDx includes sarcoidosis and SLE). Recommend repeat chest CT in 6 weeks to assess resolution of consolidation. # Asthma: Patient reported mild to moderate shortness of breath with activity on admission but described the dyspnea as different from that of her usual asthma symptoms. Mild wheezing was appreciated on exam which improved with albuterol nebulizers. She was continued on Advair and albuterol per her home regimen and did not have any other episodes of dyspnea. Chronic issues: # Hypertension: BP on admission was in the 160-180s/90s, likely due to missing an amlodipine dose and in the setting of significant pain. Her blood pressure normalized after restarting home amlodipine and pain medications. # CKD, stage 3: Creatinine remained at baseline 1.0-1.4 during admission. # Bipolar disorder: Stable, continued home Seroquel regimen. # Sarcoidosis: Stable. Patient was scheduled with Rheumatology follow-up as above. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== [ ] Patient was discharged on levofloxacin to complete a 5 day course (last day ___. [ ] Recommend repeat chest CT in 6 weeks to ensure resolution of opacity (DDx includes malignancy). If the opacity persists then would recommend lung biopsy. This was discussed with patient prior to discharge. [ ] Patient was scheduled for an appointment with Rheumatology to workup other causes of her pleuritis.
summary: You were admitted for chest pain. You were evaluated in the ED with a chest xray and CT scan, which showed no immediate life threatening conditions. The images showed an area of your left lung concerning for possible pneumonia (lung infection). You were started on a five day course of antibiotics and your pain was controlled with pain medications. You were also having pain on your right side, though there was no visible explanation for this pain on our CT scan. Sometimes this pain can happen because of inflammation of the lung lining. We have scheduled you follow-up in ___ clinic for further workup of this pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with hypertension, hypothyroidism who presented with acute onset shortness of breath and wheezing after two doses of naprosyn for right hip pain. The wheezing and shortness of breath resolved with two albuterol nebulizer treatments in the ED, per the patient. #1. SOB/Wheezing: Possible etiologies for this included infection, pulmonary embolism, airway disease such as asthma, pulmonary edema, or allergic reaction to medication. Given the patient's presentation and history, the diagnosis that considered most likely was hyperreactive airway disease secondary to naprosyn use/allergy. -Chest xray on ___ was without evidence of pneumonia. V/Q scan suggestive of airway disease and low likelihood of pulmonary embolus. There was no pleuritic component to the patient's chest pain; no history to suggest DVT. -Troponins negative x 3, no EKG changes, no chest pain throughout admission. -Patient remained afebrile, had no leukocytosis, or anything in history or exam to suggest infectious process. -Given patient's history of allergies with wheezing and the fact that he was naprosyn naive, his symptoms were deemed likely airway reactivity secondary to this medication, especially since it resolved with albuterol and with discontinuation of the medication. -Patient may benefit from pulmonary function testing as outpatient and possible consideration for antihistamine treatment and albuterol inhaler prn for wheezing-will recommend this to patient's PCP and have informed patient of same. The patient was discharged with a prescription for albuterol inhaler for use as needed for wheezing. #2. Hypertension, Hypothyroidism: we continued the patient's home medications. Code: Full (discussed with patient) The patient was deemed medically fit for discharge to home on ___. He was scheduled for close follow-up with his primary care provider, Dr. ___, for ___.
summary: you were admitted to the hospital because of shortness of breath and wheezing. We think that you experienced these symptoms because of naprosyn use during the two days prior to admission. Given your history of hypersensitivity/allergies, it is likely that the airways in your lungs reacted to this medication, causing your symptoms. It is our recommendation that you do not use naprosyn and that you avoid the use of other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin), and Aleve unless recommended by your primary care doctor, and even then with caution and attentiveness to your symptoms. We have added naprosyn as a drug allergy in your ___ medical record.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY ================= Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with hypertension, who is an active ___ PPD cigarette smoker, who presented to ED on ___ with acute dyspnea and wheezing in the setting of 1 week of of fevers, chills, cough, and runny nose. In the ED, she briefly required BiPAP and was admitted for treatment of COPD/asthma exacerbation though notably patient has no prior diagnosis of COPD/asthma. She was treated with oral prednisone, azithromycin, and inhalers, with improvement in her symptoms. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Will require PFTs to properly diagnose COPD/asthma [] Continue reinforcing smoking cessation and offer medications if necessary. Discharged on 14 mg/day patch, consider tapering to 7 mg/day in ___ weeks then off with nicotine gum if well tolerated. [] Patient reported poor adherence to amlodipine. Blood pressures were high in hospital. Would discuss importance of adherence to amlodipine and Advair with patient. ACUTE ISSUES ============ # Dyspnea # Mixed hypoxic/hypercapnic respiratory failure # Asthma vs COPD # Leukocytosis Reports ___ weeks of productive cough, fatigue, malaise, consistent with viral URI. Worsening dyspnea brought her to PCP ___ (started prednisone and Advair) and ER ___. Briefly required BiPAP in ED, and was on 4L O2 via NC upon arrival to the floor ___. Received 80 mg IV methylprednisolone, 500 mg azithromycin, and magnesium 2gm IV in ED. VBG with uncompensated respiratory acidosis. No documented history of asthma or COPD. Patient has 20 pack-year smoking history. Recent viral illness could be trigger for exacerbation of underlying COPD. CXR could not rule out early PNA although patient reports no fevers/chills. On ___, patient saturated well on room air, walked around the floor without dyspnea, clinically improved. Discharged on short prednisone burst and azithromycin for a ___dvair, and nicotine replacement therapy. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #HTN - Continued home amlodipine 5 mg, patient was taking intermittently at home, reinforced that it needs to be taken daily. #Tobacco use disorder Patient smokes <10 cigarettes a day - Started Nicotine patch 14mg/24hr, nicotine gum PRN on discharge
summary: You were in the hospital because you were having trouble breathing and were admitted for management You were treated for a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) exacerbation with steroids, antibiotics, and inhaled medications that help to open your airways and decrease inflammation. We discussed COPD with you and talked about how one of the most important and effective ways to treat it is to stop smoking. Please continue to take 40 mg prednisone and 250 mg of azithromycin once a day for the next two days. Please use your Advair inhaler twice a day, every day. Please take your 5 mg of amlodipine once a day, EVERYDAY. Please do not continue to smoke cigarettes. There are many smoking cessation tools available to you and you are encouraged to discuss them further with your primary care doctor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male w/ h/o HTN and reactive airway disease presents with HTN to 200/100, chest pain, and cough and LUQ pain with paraesophageal LN and questionable gastric wall thickening on CT. #Abdominal tenderness - Patient denied any GI symptoms upon presentation; however his exam was notable for moderate tenderness over LUQ and less so over LLQ.
