Elevated LDH and history of intermittent fevers/night sweats over the past month were concerning for potential malignancy. CT scan showed a mildly enlarged paraesophageal LN as well as possible gastric wall thickening, which may have just been due to gastric non-distention. EGD was performed on ___, which showed a 2mm polyp, clubbed antral fold, pre-pyloric erosions, all biopsied. Patient was discharged with no GI complaints and gastric biopsies pending. Because of the erosions seen on EGD, GI recommended increasing his omeprazole from 20mg to 40mg daily. #Hypertension: Pt had elevated BPs at home for the week prior to admit (occ up to ___. He continued to be markedly hypertensive despite increasing lisinopril from 20mg to 30mg. This was the primary reason for his admission. BP came down to 160/100 in ED without any intervention. Patient had no evidence of end-organ dysfunction. We increased lisinopril to 40mg and chlorthalidone to 25mg. Continued carvedilol 25mg BID. BP down to 152/86 now. After changes to his antihypertensive regimen, his BPs were consistently between 130-155/80-95. #Chest pain: Etiology was unclear. Certainly was not typical of anginal chest pain. Negative troponin, unchanged EKG, and clean cath from ___ were reassuring. It was thought most likely to be muskuloskeletal in etiology as there was tenderness to palpation over affected area. This pain was not particularly bothersome to the patient so we held off on furthur w/u. His chest pain did not change in character or severity throughout admission. He described a ___, dull, achey pain over the left anterior chest with no exacerbating/alleviating factors. On discharge, this pain was attributed to costochondritis. #Cough/wheezing: Likely ___ mild flare of patient's reactive airway disease. No evidence of respiratory distress/hypoxemia. No evidence of infection. Physical exam with minimal wheezing. Patient received one dose of methylprednisolone in the ED; however, he did not require any furthur steroids throughout his admission. He only required very rare albuterol/ipratropium MDI use. At discharge, his symptoms were at his baseline. He was discharged with Combivent to replace his previous albuterol inhaler. #GERD - Increased omeprazole from 20mg daily to 40mg daily per GI recs given evidence of erosive gastritis on EGD. ============================================================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #HTN: Patient should have close followup to ensure that up-titrated antihypertensive regimen as outlined above is effective in outpatient setting. Also, since both lisinopril and chlorthalidone were increased, he should have a repeat electrolyte panel within 1 week of discharge. #Gastric biopsies: Given CT appearance of stomach and fold clubbing seen on EGD, there is some concern for gastric malignancy. Biopsies were collected on ___ and will need to be followed up by patient's PCP and triaged according to results.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for high blood pressure and chest pain. We increased 2 of your blood pressure medications, lisinopril and chlorthalidone, and your blood pressure improved. Several different tests showed that your chest pain was not caused by a heart problem, and was likely caused by inflammation in the ribs, called costochondritis. You were also found to have some tenderness in your upper abdomen. An endoscopy was performed, which showed a small polyp, a small erosion, and a slight change in one of the folds in your stomach. The significance of these changes is unclear so biopsies were taken, and the results should be reported within ___ weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient was admitted to the MICU, where he was tachypneic and wheezing on presentation. He was initially treated with continuous albuterol nebs and IV steroids, with gradual improvement. ABG on ___ showed 197/42/7.35/42 while on continuous nebulizer treatments. He remained afebrile but in light of productive cough was started empirically on therapy for a community-acquired pneumonia with Ceftriaxone and azithromycin. He was noted to AG of undetermined etiology upon presentation, which had resolved by HD3. IV steroids were changed to PO prednisone. On the morning of ___, he had a peak flow of 500 and was transferred out of the ICU to the Medical ward team. Steroid course was planned for a two-week taper. Ceftriaxone was switched to oral Cefpodoxime with plan to complete a 7-day course of cephalosporin and a 5-day course of azithromycin. . Medical floor course: . ___ y/o gentleman with Asthma and Hepatitis C cirrhosis presented to the ED with asthma exacerbation, initially admitted to MICU now transferred with improved respiratory status. . # Asthma Exacerbation. Patient was much improved by ___ with PF in 550s and no subjective senation of dyspnea. Based on further interview, it appears that exacerbation was triggered by known culprit exposures (auto exhaust, cigarrette smoke, spices) and possible URI. It was felt that exacerbation was unlikely due to CAP, given no fever, clear sputum and no opacities on CXR. No sputum data was available. Patient was discontinued from cefpodoxime and completed course of Azithromycin. Pt. also had PE findings consistent with allergic rhinitis. He was continued on albuterol nebs Q2H prn, ipratropium nebs q6h, prednisone 60mg. With this treatment he continued to improve with PF of 650 on ___. Based on this response, he was discharged home with a 2wk prednisone taper, continued on Advair and singulair. Given findings consistent with allergic rhinitis, patient was started on loratadine at discharged and instructed to resume flonase after completion of PO prednisone. He was also provided with clotrimazole troches tid prn for hx of oral candidiasis with previous prednisone treatments. He was also treated with HISS for high dose steroids with ___ ranging 106 - 175. Patient was also referred to Allergy clinic to further assess for asthma triggers. . # Hep C cirrhosis: nonresponder to prior treatment as per last ___. No stigmata of chronic liver disease on exam, but last RUQ u/s consistent with cirrhosis. Not an active issue during this admission. Patient is to followup with Dr. ___ as outpatient. . # Hypercalcemia. Appears chronic at least from ___ from OMR Total Ca > 11. Alb was 4.0, Phosphate 2.4, low. PTH elevated at 153, and Mr. ___ had normal renal function. Hypercalcemia was confirmed with iCa value of 1.50. Patient did report having vague abdominal pain over the past ___ years, as well as fatigue and paresthesias on/off in ___ of the same duration. No other complaints consistent w/ symptomatic hypercalcemia. This was felt to most likely represent primary Hyperpara, given elevated PTH and nl renal function. Can also be related to chronic vitamin D defficiency, and a 1,25 Vitamin D level was sent prior to discharge. This work up was communicated with PCP via email. Patient may require 24hr calcium and bone density measurements as OP as well as parathyroid imaging. ___ need to consider Raloxefine/bisphosphonate based on above results. . # HTN. SBPs 130 - 140s. Monitored during admission. . # PPX: sc heparin, can d/c once patient ambulated tid. continue protonix given high dose steroids. . # CODE STATUS: Full Code, confirmed with patient . # Contact: Daughter ___ ___, Father ___ ___ . Patient was discharged with improved breathing, peak flows and with mild cough with appropriate follow up.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with a severe asthma exacerbation (shortness of breath, cough). It was felt that this was due to exposure to triggers known to you (e.g. car exhaust, cigarette smoke, spices) as well as a possible infection. For this you required high dose steroids, nebulizers and treated with antibiotics. You did not require intubation but did require intensive care unit stay. You will require to continue the steroids for a total of two weeks (see below). You will also requrie to take one more day of antibiotics (Azithromycin). In addition, you should also continue to take your flovent on daily basis and albuterol as needed. Finally because evidence of allergic rhinitis (allergies) were noted on your exam, you should continue to take singulair and you were started on loratadine (claritin). After you complete the prednisone tablet course, you should resume the use of your flonase on a regular, daily basis. In addition, you were found to have a high calcium blood level. Evaluation showed that this may be due to a gland in your neck.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of severe asthma and recurrent URI over the past few months presenting with dyspnea and wheezing in the setting of pharyngitis and hoarseness. # Asthma exacerbation: Patient with decreased peak flow, symptomatic dyspnea and requiring frequent nebulizer treatments. Required stacked nebs and IV magnesium in the ED. She was started on frequent albuterol nebulizers, prednisone burst and given augmentin to treat URI (see below, likely trigger). On the day of discharge she was able to ambulate without dyspnea and was requiring nebulizers no more frequently than every 4 hours. She was discharged with instructions to taper her prednisone and given an albuterol rescue inhaler. She was also set up to see new Asthma Specialist as outpatient. # Pharyngitis: patient with recurrent pharyngitis and URI over the past several months. Reportedly her ENT exam was normal earlier this month, and she was diagnosed with glosspharyngeal neuralgia. She met ___ CENTOR criteria, and has been treated with multiple antibiotics, so she is unlikely to have GAS. Also with negative monospot as outpatient. Recent throat culture with beta strep, not GAS. She has been on steroids off and on, and uses ICS, so she could be considered somewhat immunocompromised in that area, but there was no obvious infection. She does cite issues with tonsilloliths over the past months, which can be evaluated further as outpatient with ENT. She was continued on augmentin with instructions to finish augmentin course started by outpatient ENT, and to stop valacylcovir as there was no obvious viral component. She was given prednisone burst as above, and connected to outpatient ENT where she can obtain second opinion on utility of tonsillectomy. # Leukocytosis: Presented with leukocytosis to 11.3. Likely secondary to steroid use vs. acute infection. Resolved during hospitalization. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== - continue prednisone with taper (40mg ___, 30mg ___, 20mg ___, 10mg ___ - continue augmentin for total of 7 days - follow-up with outpatient ENT as scheduled
summary: You were admitted for difficulty breathing likely due to an asthma exacerbation. You were given frequent nebulizers, prednisone and continued on antibiotics. We think this was likely triggered by an upper respiratory tract infection. For this, we kept you on augmentin, which was started by our patient ENT. We stopped the valacyclovir because we did not think there was a viral infection. We are providing you with a rescue inhaler to use if you do not have your nebulizer with you. Also, we recommend that you complete the course of augmentin prescribed to you by your ENT physician. We have recommended that you taper the prednisone over the course of a few days: On ___, take 40mg. On ___, take 30mg. On ___, take 20mg. On ___, take 10mg. We have set you up with a new Asthma Specialist at ___ and ENT physician as well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ lady with recent discharge for PNA who presented w/ 3 days of SOB and 1 day of chest pain that was initially concerning for unresolved pneumonia but her exam and studies were reassuring that this represented post-infectious pneumonia as well as musculoskeletal shoulder pain so she was discharged home. # Right chest pain: musculoskeletal. She presented with right-sided chest pain that was focal and reproducible with light palpation on exam. The pain is likely due to myofascial pain syndrome given her hx and relief with NSAIDs. Given recent negative work-up for rheumatological and infectious causes, we did not perform repeat CK, ___ and ___ testing. Prior work-up showed elevated CRP and ESR. Shoulder joint pain was considered due to equivocal shoulder impingement testing and poor active range of motion. ACS is unlikely given negative Troponin x 2 and normal ECG. Also unlikley to be pericarditis due to focal pain and negative pericardial rub on exam. She was treated with Naproxen and Morphine and experienced some relief. # Shortness of breath: Reactive airways. Given her recent history of PNA 10 days PTA, her symptoms were initially concerning for recurrent or undertreated pneumonia. However, her CXR is not worsened from prior and she has been afebrile. Due to her hx of Asthma, wheezing on exam, and relief of dyspnea with Albuterol, she likely has a Post-pneumonia reactive airway disease. We also considered chronic eosinophilic pneumonia given the peripheral eosinophilia but were unimpressed by the CXR findings. SOB is not likely to be PE given no leg swelling and nl O2 sat on RA. She was initially given Ceftriaxone 1g IV and Azithromycin 500mg IV in the ED but that was subsequently discontinued due to low likelihood of pneumonia. She was encouraged to use her home albuterol which she reported improved the dyspnea. # Myofascial pain syndrome: chronic. Patient has a hx of myofascial pain syndrome and currently reports diffuse muscular pain which was reproducible on exam with light palpation. A rheum work-up for inflammatory arthritis was negative during her last admission in ___ except for elevated CRP and ESR. We controlled her pain with NSAIDs and Morphine and increased the frequency of Gabapentin to 300mg TID. On discharge, we added Acetaminophen, Ibuprofen, and increased frequency of Gabapentin to 300mg TID. # Hypertension: pt has a hx of HTN and was stable with SBP in 110s during admission. She was continued on her home medication of Amlodipine 1 mg qd and HCTZ 25MG QD # Allergic Rhinitis: stable. She reported increased post-nasal drip. She was instructed to follow the Loratadine 10mg qd prescription. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #. Pending at the time of discharge: None # Code: Full (discussed with patient) # Communication: Patient # Emergency Contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted with cough, shortness of breath, and chest wall pain and were admitted because there was concern that your previous pneumonia might not have been adequately treated. Your history, exam, labs, and imaging were reassuring. Your cough and shortness of breath are likely related to your asthma. Please continue to take your Fluticasone inhaler twice a day as prescribed (even if you do not have symptoms). Then you can use the Albuterol as needed. It is VERY IMPORTANT that you stop smoking completely, as this can cause your breathing to become much worse and also increases your risk of lung cancer. As for your chest wall pain, we made sure you were not having a heart attack. On your previous admission, you were ruled out for inflammatory causes of pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/oF with subacute worsening of shortness of breath likely multifactorial related to volume overload and reactive airway disease and restrictive physiology. # Dyspnea: Likely a component of volume overload especially given recent diuretic reduction coupled with bronchospasm from underlying reactive airway disease. CTA on admission negative for PE. Patient initially diuresed with IV lasix and then transitioned to outpatient PO regimen of 80mg daily (on last hospital admission she had been discharged on furosemide twice weekly). Cardiology consulted given concern for managing volume overload in setting of severe AS and tenuous fluid balance. Cardiology suggested continuing oral furosemide regimen and no further diuresis as patient's residual dyspnea likely related more to reactive airway disease. Patient seen by pulmonary consult team for reactive airway disease management and they recommended continued use of atrovent, holding albuterol, reducing prednisone to outpatient regimen of 10mg daily and getting outpt sleep study given strong suspicion for OSA. Pulmonary also recommended titrating O2 to keep sats >93%. Patient sating in high 90's on 2L at time of discharge. Would suggest continuing to titrte down O2 to keep sats greater than 93%. Outpatient sleep study ordered--patient should be contacted by sleep lab for appt. Would eventually transition patient from nebulized ipratropium to inhaler form and discharge her on this medication. # UTI: Patient found to have ESBL E.Coli. Started on IV Meropenem. She should complete a 14 day course to end ___. # AFib: Patient remained rate controlled on outpatient regimen of metoprolol 50mg BID. Warfarin reduced to 1mg daily. This is a warfarin dose reduction (she was taking ___ daily) thus INR should be monitored closely and adjusted as necessary to maintain INR ___. # Acute Renal Failure: Likely pre-renal in setting of giving IV lasix. Renal function improved to Cr of 1 after changing patient back to her outpatient regimen of furosemide 80mg daily. # S/P Left Hip Fixation for Femur Fx: Surgery on ___. Staples removed during this hospitalization and post-op femur xray checked and looked good. Patient should follow up with ortho in 4 weeks. She can continue taking tylenol for pain control. # RA/SLE: Prednisone reduced to 10mg daily. Patient should continue prophylactic dose of Bactrim until ___ as this reduced dose does not require PCP ___. # Glaucoma: Continued on home eye drops # Peripheral neuropathy: Continued gabapentin # Anxiety: Continued outpatient Lorazepam. # GERD: Continued omeprazole. CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath which we believe was related to fluid overload and your underlying lung disease. You were restarted on your regular dosing of lasix. You were seen by the cardiology team who believe your aortic stenosis is stable and do not believe that this is the major contributor to your breathing problems. You were also seen by the pulmonary doctors who suggested that you start a new inhaler called ipratropium, stop using albuterol and have an outpatient sleep study. An order has been placed for this study and you should be contacted by the sleep lab. You also were found to have a urinary tract infection and were started on IV antibiotics. You were seen by the orthopedic surgery team who removed your staples.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ y/o female with a history of asthma, HTN, CKD who presented from her PCP's office with SOB and wheezing. #SOB/Wheezing: Patient presented following two weeks of productive cough (yellow sputum) and several days of SOB and wheezing. Initial exam notable for diffuse wheezes. CXR was normal. She was treated with duoneb x2 and prednisone 60 mg x1 with improvement in her breathing and wheezing. Subsequently, prednisone was discontinued (after two doses) and she was transitioned to albuterol inhaler q6h and advair BID (formulary equivalent of home symbicort). DVT US was also done given ___ edema; there was no evidence of DVT. She remained on room air throughout the admission and had a normal respiratory exam without wheezes on day of discharge. Discharged on her home medications. Also given a peak flow monitor and encouraged to use this, record her numbers, and bring them to next PCP ___. Peak flow at the time of discharge: 230. #Deconditioning: Patient was seen by physical therapy who felt she could use outpatient physical therapy to improve her balance and minimize fall risk. No changes were made to home medication regimen. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] Peak flow at discharge: 230 [ ] Discharged on home albuterol inhaler with spacer and Symbicort. [ ] Inpatient physical therapist recommended outpatient ___ for deconditioning. #Contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted due to increasing shortness of breathing, wheezing, and coughing. Initially we did an xray of your lungs, which looked normal. We also gave you nebulizer (breathing) treatments and oral steroids for a day to help your breathing. This seemed to help. We then switched you back over to all of your home medicines (albuterol, Symbicort), and you did well while taking these. We believe this could have been a mild asthma exacerbation on top of an upper respiratory tract infection (i.e. a cold). Continue using your symbicort twice a day and use the albuterol inhaler as needed for shortness of breath or wheezing. Measure your peak flow (machine you blow into) once a day for the next ___ days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman w/PMHx blindness, ___ ___, hypothyroidism, reactive airway disease presented to the ED today with productive cough p/w worsening symptoms, increased fatigue, malaise, weakness. # Reactive airway disease # Probable bronchospasm related to recent virus. Patient completed 10 day course of levaquin for bronchitis. Patient has a history of reactive airway disease. Recently saw PCP and was prescribed fluticasone and albuterol for treatment which she has not used. On exam, lungs sound clear, mild crackles at base, no wheezing and moving air well, no signs of tachypnea or respiratory distress. CXR negative for infection. She was treated symptomatically with cough suppressants and nebulizer treatments. She was given fluticasone/salmeterol BID in place of her home inhaler, which was non-formulary. # Systolic Murmur: Subtle, likely secondary to mild aortic stenosis. Echo shows mild MR. #A Fib: EKG in ER shows new A Fib, repeat and 24hrs on telemetry all NSR. Echo without evidence of significant valvular disease. Low CHADS and history of subdural as well as blindness and questions about safe, independent home living make anticoagulation risky relative to odds of stroke. Recommendation was discussed with the patient, who was in agreement. # Failure to thrive: Patient with increasing difficulties at her assisted living facility, has not taken her medications for 3 weeks, presented to urgent care without pants, is blind at baseline, started small fire in her apartment. Physical therapy recommended rehab. # Paranoid delusions: Patient with history of paranoid delusions, thinks people are breaking into her apartment, changes her locks and locks herself into rooms. Patient follows with a psychiatrist Dr. ___ of ___ and was prescribed 2 mg Haldol daily, however patient has not taken her medications in 3 weeks and her delusions have worsened per daughter without the ___. She was intermittently compliant with medication in house. Her outpatient psychiatrist was contacted and expressed similar concerns. He will work with the rehab team in continuing this assessment and working with the patient and family to determine whether it will ultimately be possible for the patient to safely transfer back to her independent living situation. # Blindness: she continues to have release symptoms from her blindness from macular degeneration, sees a white donut shape like figure in the left side, which is nearly constant. # Hypothyroidism: TSH 1.0 wnl. home dose of levothyroxine was continued # Osteoporosis/prolonged bisphosphonate therapy: previously treated with bisphosonates, no treatment for several years. # H/O SDH ___ c/b seizure activity: traumatic subdural hematoma with resultant probable focal seizures, previously on keppra, tapered off at the end of ___ given EEG was negative for seizure activity TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #Continue discussion of risks and benefits of anticoagulation with paroxysmal a fib #Continue to assess patient's ability to safely transition home with patient and family. If patient cannot safely transition home, family may need to seek guardianship. #Monitor medication compliance, patient frequently declines meds, particularly her psychiatric prescriptions. #Consider head imaging, given history of falls and intracranial hemorrhage, if etiology of declining mental status is in question
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of fatigue following a recent bout of pneumonia. We did not find any evidence of another infection or for inadequate treatment of your pneumonia. You improved significantly and felt very well by your second day in the hospital We had our physical therapists evaluate you and they felt that you would benefit from rehabilitation to improve your strength and balance. We also found that you had atrial fibrillation when you were in the emergency room. As we discussed, this is an abnormal heart rhythm, that people can go in and out of and that increases your risk of stroke. We discussed starting a blood thinner and felt that at the present time your risk of a bleeding complication from a blood thinner outweighs your risk of stroke. You should continue this conversation with your outpatient doctors.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx severe COPD and HTN who presents with 2d of cough and shortness of breath consistent with a COPD exacerbation. #COPD exacerbation: Patient initially required frequent nebulizer treatments in the ED and was admitted to the ICU. By the time she was in the ICU, she was not in distress. She was started on treatment of COPD with prednisone 60mg and azithromycin 500mg x1 then 250mg daily along with frequent nebulized treatments. CXR showed no focal infiltrate. She was transfered to the floor the following day. She had complaints of sore throat on admission with cough/secretions - possibly consistent with viral URI. Other potential exacerbating factors are possibly allergies (during this time of the season), postnasal gtt, and GERD symptoms. She has a baseline severe COPD/Chronic asthma with PFT FEV1 0.66 (41%) and reversal airway component (>12% increase in FEV1, FVC with bronchodilators), so I felt that along with the usual COPD treatments, the reactive airway component (and any exacerbating factors) should also be treated aggressively. She was treated with duonebs ATC and then PRN, advair, azithro, singulair, and prednisone. Afrin was given PRN for postnasal gtt, prilosec increased to 40 mg BID for likely GERD component. She had an allergic reaction to cetirizine and thus an allergy medication was not initiated during this stay. Zyrtec may be considered as an outpt. She was also given codeine for cough suppression as this might be precipitating bronchospasm. He was then transitioned to her home spiriva and weaned of oxygen. She was able to ambulate (with walker) and keep O2 sats above 90%. She was cleared by ___ to go home (with home ___. She was recommended to follow with her PCP in the following ___ weeks. # HTN: mildly hypertensive during this hospitalization (SBP 160). She was continued on clonidine, hydralazine. HCTZ was increased to 25 mg daily. # FEN: diet, replete electrolytes # Prophylaxis: - DVT: pneumoboots, heparin BID - GI: omeprazole # Access: PIVs # Communication: son ___ ___, son ___ ___, or ___ ___ # Code: DNR/DNI
summary: ___. As you know, you were admitted for shortness of breath consistent with worsening asthma. You were given multiple treatments in the intensive care unit and this was continued on the regular medicine floors. A chest x-ray here showed no evidence of pneumonia. We believe that the asthma exacerbation was likely due to a number of factors: possible a cold (viral infection), allergy, and acid reflux. You are given medications to assist with improving your breathing. Please be sure that your house does not have any mold (during this summer months) that can possibly worsen your breathing. Also avoiding places with a lot of smoking would also help.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of hypertension, mild intermittent asthma, severe eczema on cyclosporine who presents with worsening dyspnea x 1 week. . # PUMP: Pt found to have cardiomyopathy with moderate systolic (EF 30%) and severe diastolic dysfunction. Etiology unknown and chronicity unclear, but enlarged atria/left ventricle by ECG and confirmed on echo suggests that this development is not acute. Possible this is secondary to hypertension, however blood pressure not elevated and given that patient so young this seems less likely. Cardiomyopathy may be viral in etiology, may have been exacerbated by or initiation of cyclosporine. Patient was on discharge. No significant travel history to support Lyme or Chagas disease, no recent history of heavy drinking to suggest EtOH-induced cardiomyopathy. Pt with improvement in dyspnea with diuresis, afterload reduction, and blood pressure control; metoprolol switched to carvedilol, started lisinopril and carvedilol, and discontinued cyclosporine. Pt to follow up with Dr. ___ on ___. Patient got cardiac MRI today which will be folloewd up by Dr. ___ at her appointment on ___. Will need repeat echo after discharge as per cardiology; further evaluation may include biopsy to rule out sarcoidosis or amyloidosis. . # CORONARIES: Pt ruled out for MI. Antihypertensives uptitrated and cyclosporine discontinued for secondary prevention with adequate BP control. . # RHYTHM: Monitored on telemetry with no rhythm abnormalities noted. . # Hypertension: Blood pressures markedly elevated in ED. Per patient, baseline hypertension had been exacerbated by initiation of cyclosporine. Cyclosporine discontinued - Derm agreeable, and patient started on metoprolol, aldactone, lisinopril and lasix with improved BP control. . # RLL pneumonia: Small RLL infiltrate on admission CXR which could represent early pneumonia. Given dose of levofloxacin in ED but stopped on floor as pt afebrile, without leukocytosis, and found to have cardiomyopathy. However, as cough persistant and sputum noted to be more yellow/green; CXR with some question of RLL infiltrate so pt was started on 5-day course of azithromycin; may have additional benefit of decreasing airway inflammation. . # Mild intermittent asthma: Well controlled in past and not requiring albuterol at baseline. Wheezing heard on admission but not subsequently. However, still pt reported feeling wheezy, relieved somewhat with ipratropium and albuterol nebs. Suspect that much of the wheezing secondary to cardiac asthma from fluid overload. Patient advised not to use inhalers too much as albuterol may cause tachycardia which can worsen her cardiomyopathy. . # Acute renal failure: Cr 1.2 on admission likely ___ decreased forward flow in setting of cardiomyopathy, resolved with diuresis. . # Diarrhea: Given she is a ___ and recent antibiotic use, C. diff checked and was negative. Diarrhea resolved during hospitalization, may have been ___ cyclosporine. . # Eczema: Followed by Derm here. Pt recently started on cyclosporine for severe eczema after failing or refusing other treatments. Cyclosporine stopped as was contributing to hypertension; other outpatient topical medications continued. Derm evaluated pt and agreed with plan. Scheduled to follow up with Dr. ___ on ___ to discuss alternate medical therapies for treatment of severe eczema - Derm consult recommended Imuran as possibility. . # Elevated AST: AST mildly elevated at 66 on admission, may have been ___ to congestive hepatopathy. Returned to normal when rechecked a few days later. . # Iron deficiency anemia: ___ be related to fibroids. Pt had been on iron supplements in past but discontinued, restarted on this admission. . # Fibroids: Followed by Ob-Gyn. Continued on outpatient Provera, scheduled to follow up with Dr. ___ on ___. . # Insomnia: Pt given trazadone 50 mg qhs prn, which she tolerated well.
summary: You were admitted for increasing shortness of breath. Your echocardiogram showed decreased heart pump function with an ejection fraction of 30%. You are still completing work-up for the cause of your cardiomyopathy. You are getting your cardiac MRI today on discharge and will follow-up for this with Dr. ___ ___ Cardiology. Please avoid alcohol as it may worsen your heart function. In addition, we would reccomend that you avoid overusing your asthma inhaler as this may actually be worsening your cardiomyopathy. Your cyclosporine was discontinued as it was contributing to you high blood pressure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with history of anxiety, substance abuse, and asthma presenting with three weeks of strange symptoms and multiple ___ evals. 1) Chills/ Warm Sensation/ Paresthesias/ Paresthesias/ Presyncope: The patient presented for at least his fourth visit for a complicated set of somatic symptoms including feeling hot or cold, migrating paresthesias, fecal urgency, and presyncope. His febrile symptoms are, of course, concerning for infection and his neurological symptoms and fever raise concern for neurogenic or cardiogenic syncope vs seizure. The patient was monitored >24 hours and had multiple episodes of this symptom complex. Vital signs were checked numerous times and he was monitored on telemetry. There were no episodes of concerning arrhythmia, hemodynamic instability, fevers, chills, or other appreciable physical exam finding with the exception of the patient being somewhat diaphoretic and having some mild, diffuse tenderness to palpation on his abdomen. A wide range of labs were checked and no significant abnormalities were detected. Ultimate diagnosis remains unclear though the team has considerable suspicion of anxiety or atypical panic attacks given the patient's marked anxiety and the lack of physical findings. Other possible etiologies would be vasovagal (near) syncope perhaps initiated by a viral syndrome that caused loose stools and nausea (though neither of these GI symptoms were reported during this hospitalization). His CT head was benign and pretest probability of stroke or severe intracranial process with NO hard neurological deficits on exam in this young, healthy man would be very low. Other much less likely diagnostic possibilities would be pheochromocytoma or carcinoid, both of which can cause flushing spells (carcinoid would better explain fecal urgency) though one would expect at least some vital sign abnormalities during the episodes. Ultimately, the very benign nature of the tests and data from monitoring were discussed with the patient and Dr ___ at ___ ___. Given the difficulties with initiating 24 hour urine monitoring and sending send-out tests in an ultimately VERY stable looking man tests of 5-HIAA and urine metanephrines were deferred. We explained to the patient that though we were unable to give a specific diagnosis findings were suggestive of something not immediately dangerous, which he found reassuring. The patient was discharged to follow up with his PCP for further ___ of these symptoms. 2) Wheezing/ SOB: Patient has history of asthma and was wheezing on presentation. He was given albuterol inhaler in house. 3) History of substance abuse: He was continued on his home buprenorphine-naloxone. He tolerated a full diet. He received subcutaneous heparin for DVT prophylaxis. He was full code.
summary: You were admitted due to concerns about the symptoms you have been having including sensation of hot and cold, urgency to go to the bathroom, abdominal discomfort, shortness of breath, and feeling as if you might pass out. You were admitted and monitored overnight. You have had no vital sign abnormalities, dangerous cardiac rhythms, and your labs were unremarkable. A CT scan showed no major intracranial process. It is unclear what is causing your symptoms. Most likely you had a viral syndrome that exacerbated your asthma and perhaps is causing something called vasovagal episodes, during which you may feel lightheaded. There are a few other rare illnesses that could cause some of these symptoms but these require 24 hour urine corrections and take some time to return.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of COPD/asthma (not on home O2), recent PE on warfarin, mood disorder, chronic abdominal pain, chronic constipation and gastritis, who presents with progressively worsening shortness of breath over the past 6 days. # ACUTE COPD EXACERBATION: Patient with gradual onset SOB, extremely musical and wheezing throughout all lung fields, increased sputum production, peak flows extremely depressed. No oxygen requirement. Low suspicion for PE despite cp symptoms and subthereapeutic INR as patient bridged with lovenox and negative LENIs. Unlikely CHF exacerbation given low BNP. Patient ruled out for ACS. Patient treated with duonebs and albuterol, advair and spiriva. Patient received 125 solumedrol in ___. Patient started on prednisone burst. Azithromycin deferred given no radiographic evidence of pneumonia. Unknown trigger -- patient to follow up with PCP as outpatient. Consider outpatient pulmonology for better baseline control. # Abdominal Pain: Patient with known chronic abdominal pain. Pain controlled with pain control as recorded by PCP's not without escalation. Patient with mild transaminitis on admission. RUQ u/s negative for acute abnormalities. Physical exam benign. LFTs downtrended - likely elevated in the setting of nausea/vomiting, which also resolved upon discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES # h/o PE: Acute pulmonary embolism in ___ WITH second episode, warrants lifelong anticoagulation. No evidence of PE this admission. Patient bridged with Lovenox. Received 7.5 mg warfarin on ___, 5mg ___, 5mg ___. Patient to follow up with ___ clinic for further titration. # Mood disorder/Psych: Continued home carbamazapine, duloxetine, quetiapine, propranalol, trazodone qhs # Tobacco Abuse: Continued nicotine patch # Chronic joint pains: continued percocet Rx as prescribed by PCP # Migraines: continued triptan prn # Bowels: continued home bowel regimen TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -------------------- [] PCP: ___ up COPD control [] PCP: ___ on prednisone taper (___) [] PCP: ___ on warfarin. Patient bridged with lovenox. Management per ___ clinic.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath, due to a COPD exacerbation. Your Peak flows upon discharge are still lower than expected. Please continue to take your nebulizer treatment and albuterol treatments at home. Please arrange to see your primary care provider, Dr. ___, ___ your COPD control. As your COPD is poorly controlled at baseline, he may recommend that you see a pulmonologist (lung doctor). Please take your prednisone for two more days. DO NOT TAKE ENOXAPARIN AFTER DISCHARGE UNLESS DIRECTED by ___ ___. Continue to work with the ___ ___ clinic for appropriate titration of your warfarin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ h/o episode thought to be myocarditis in ___ (with EF 40% initially, which recovered to 60%), who presented with chest pain/pressure and dyspnea which she described as similar to the episode in ___. #Chest pain/pressure/dyspnea: Troponin was elevated, and pt underwent cardiac catheterization, which showed no evidence of coronary artery disease. She then had an echo and cardiac MRI consistent with an atypical variant of Takotsubo's, with hypokinesis of the midanteroseptal and inferolateral walls. No effusion. MRI showed no enhancement/scar/evidence of myocarditis. EF on the echo was 40%, with MRI the next day showing EF 55%, with less hypokinesis seen on MRI than on echo, consistent w/ interval improvement. Pt also noted an improvement of symptoms, with no chest pain on day of discharge, no dyspnea at rest, and DOE much improved. On admission, pt was initially started on ibuprofen and colchicine, for potential myocarditis/pericarditis. However, as MRI was more consistent with stress cardiomyopathy, pt was discharged off those medications. Pt was continued on home aspirin 81 and metoprolol succinate 25mg daily, and lisinopril 2.5mg daily was started. #History of exercise-induced Asthma: Has not needed inhaler for running in long time. Transitional issues: - Pt should avoid strenuous physical activity such as running until being cleared by cardiology - Pt should continue her home metoprolol. She was also started on 2.5mg lisinopril daily. #Full code # CONTACT: Patient, husband ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN wheezing 2. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 3. Metoprolol Succinate XL 25 mg PO DAILY 4. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and chest pain. Your labs showed elevated cardiac enzymes, which can be a sign of injury to your heart. Therefore, you had a cardiac catheterization to rule out a heart attack as the cause of those elevated enzymes. On the catheterization, blood flow to your heart looked very good, with no narrowing of the arteries - you did not appear to have had a heart attack. You then had an echo and a cardiac MRI - both tests were most consistent with a diagnosis of stress cardiomyopathy, also known as Takotsubo's. Your MRI, done the day after the echo, already showed some improvement in your cardiac function.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH significant for asthma presents with worsening cough, wheezing and shortness of breath. # Asthma exacerbation: The patient's presentation was consistent with her prior asthma exacerbations. This episode was likely triggered by a viral URI/bronchitis. It was unclear why her current exacerbation persisted beyond her usual course of 2 days. CXR was negative for pneumonia. CT chest showed mild air trapping and subsegmental atelectasis. Because of her prolonged course the patient was treated empirically for atypical pneumonia for 5 days. She was placed on standing albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, as well as advair. She will complete a 10 day course of prednisone 60mg. The patient still complained of dyspnea on the day of discharge, though much improved from her admission symptoms. She never required supplemental oxygen. Peak flows on day of discharge ranged from 400-500cc, which is well above her baseline. # Leukocytosis: The patient's WBC trended up during hospitalization. She only complained of cough. She was never febrile and did not have any new infectious symptoms. CXR only showed a band of atelecatasis, and CT chest was negative for pneumonia. We felt her leukocytosis was most likely due to steroids. # Chest pain: The patient complained of intermittent chest pain, localized to her sternum. The patient described chest tightness. At times, her pain was reproducible with palpation. EKG showed new T wave inversion in V2 and T-wave flattening in V3. Troponins were negative x 2. Repeat EKG showed resolution of T-wave abnormalities. It was felt her chest pain was multifactorial: musculoskeletal after recent URI and from asthma. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient completed a 5 day course of azithromycin. - Patient was started on prednisone 60mg for a 10 day course (end date ___. - Should patient's symptoms persist, consider referral to Pulmonary. - Patient mentioned abnormal sensation in her lower abdomen. Urine hCG was negative. Consider further workup as an outpatient. - CT chest showed a thyroid lesion, most likely a cyst. Consider ultrasound as an outpatient. - CT chest showed increased soft tissue in the right breast. Consider clinical assessment to determine if patient should have a mammogram done.
summary: As you recall you were admitted for an asthma exacerbation, which was likely triggered by a recent viral infection. We obtained a CT image of your chest, which appeared normal. We wil make sure your primary care physician is aware of the thyroid and breast findings, and these can be followed up as an outpatient. We treated you albuterol nebulizers every four hours and ipratropium every six hours. We treated you with an antibiotic, azithromycin, for five days for a possible infection. You were also started on steroids (prednisone 60mg). You should continue this for a 10 day course (end date ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ ___ woman with pmhx of asthma and sarcoidosis, presented to the ___ ED complaining of shortness of breath and wheezing. In the ED, her initial VS were 97.2, 89, 173/89, 22, 98%RA. Labs were notable for normal WBC, Cr 1.2 (baseline 0.9). A CXR was neg for an acute process. The patient was given duonebs and dexamethasone for suspected asthma exacerbation. Peak flow was 220 after 3 neb treatments, so the patient was admitted to medicine for further evaluation. On transfer to the floor, the patient appeared mildly uncomfortable, though she spoke in full sentences with good air movement. She received two rounds of duonebs. During her hospitalization, Ms. ___ received two doses of Prednisone 60mg IV, before being transitioned to PO Prednisone on ___. She used ipratroprium/albuterol nebulizer treatments as needed. She was maintained on her home asthma medications. With this regimen, her peak flow increased to 400. Ms. ___ also reported improvement of symptoms, with decreased chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion. She continued to have normal oxygen saturations on room air. Ms. ___ was discharged home on ___, with planned follow up with her Primary Care and Pulmonary doctors.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital on ___ for an acute exacerbation of your asthma. You were treated with nebulizer treatments (albuterol/ipratropium) and IV (intravenous) Prednisone, and then Prednisone pills. With this treatment, your Peak Flow increased from 220 to 400. You will be discharged on oral prednisone 60 mg daily. This medication dose will be decreased over time. Your primary care doctor, ___ manage this. Please resume your normal outpatient treatment regimen for asthma. We have not made any changes to your medications, except to increase the dose of prednisone. We encourage you to participate in your normal activities as much as you feel able to.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with newly diagnosed diffuse large B cell lymphoma, admitted for further evaluation of diffuse watery diarrhea, staging, and initiation of chemotherapy. . #. Diffuse Large B Cell Lymphoma: The patient has diffuse disease, and PET CT on ___ demonstrated splenic and bone marrow involvement, as well as involvement of right axillary, left paraaortic, and left inguinal nodes. She was initially started on rituximab on ___, but the infusion was stopped when the patient developed wheezing and rigors. She was treated with diphenhydramine, hydrocortisone, albuterol nebs, and inhaled racemic epinephrine, with resolution of symptoms. She then received rituximab again on ___, infused over a slower rate, and tolerated the medication well. Following rituximab, she received a chemotherapy regimen with cytoxan, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (CHOP). She tolerated this regimen well. However, during the infusion of the doxorubicin, she had extravasation of the medication after ___ were injected. She developed some erythema and ecchymosis in the area (left forearm), but did not develop tenderness, induration, or ulceration in the area. She received one dose of dexrazoxane, the antidote given in doxorubicin extravasation. She was seen by the plastic surgery team prior to discharge, who did not feel there was need for intervention. The patient will follow-up in plastic surgery clinic following discharge. She will also follow-up with her primary oncologist, Dr. ___ in ___ clinic the day following discharge, ___, at which time she will receive Neulasta. Her cell counts were closely monitored during the admission, as were tumor lysis labs. She will continue to talk allopurinol daily after discharge, and will also take prednisone for one additional day to complete 5 total days of prednisone. She was given prescriptions for ondansetron, prochlorperazine, and lorazepam to take as needed for nausea. . #. Anemia: The patient's anemia is in setting of bone marrow involvement of lymphoma. The patient received transfusion with 2 units PRBCs during the admission, and HCT was stable prior to discharge. She will need cell counts monitored as an outpatient given recent chemotherapy. The patient developed some increased pulmonary congestion in the setting of receiving the blood transfusion, and her symptoms improved with diuresis. . #. Diarrhea: The patient underwent colonoscopy on ___, which showed diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon, but was otherwise a normal colonoscopy to the level of the mid-transverse colon. The patient will still need a full screening colonoscopy done as an outpatient. All biopsies taken during colonoscopy were unremarkable. CMV culture was pending at time of discharge, but no CMV had been isolated to date. Of note, stool also tested negative for C. diff during the admission. Her diarrhea had improved prior to discharge. . #. Fever: Likely secondary to lymphoma, and there was no evidence of infection during the admission. All blood cultures drawn were negative, or negative to date, at time of discharge. She was given acetaminophen prn pain/fever, and received IVF in setting of persistent fevers and insensible fluid losses. Her fevers had resolved prior to discharge. She was not neutropenic, and was not started on antibiotics during this hospital course. . #. Esophageal thickening: Esophageal thickening had been noted on recent CT scan prior to admission, and the patient has known GERD and a hiatal hernia. She underwent an EGD on ___, which showed polyps in the stomach body and erythema in the antrum of the stomach, but was otherwise normal to the level of the third part of the duodenum. Biopsies were normal. The patient was continued on omeprazole. . #. HTN: Continued home dose atenolol. . #. Depression: Continued home dose citalopram. . #. Seasonal allergies/mild asthma: Continued flovent.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for further evaluation and treatment of your lymphoma. You had a colonoscopy and endoscopy, which showed benign polyps in your stomach and did not reveal any abnormalities. You also had PET CT scan, which shows the lymphoma involves your bone marrow, spleen and lymph nodes. You received chemotherapy with rituximab, cytoxan, doxorubicin, and vincristine. You also were started on a 5-day course of prednisone. The last day will be ___. You initially had a reaction the rituximab, but were able to tolerate the medication the following day when it was given more slowly. You also had a reaction in your skin to the doxorubicin. The plastic surgery team saw you, and would like you to follow-up in their clinic on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with history of asthma, DM2, HTN who presents with SOB, some chest tightness, wheezing, consistent with asthma exacerbation. Shortness of breath: Pt's shortness of breath, wheezing, and chest tightness in setting of recent URI likely suggestive of asthma exacerbation. She was given albuterol and prednisone coarse. Prior CXR from earlier this week showed ? RLL pneumonia, so pt was started on levofloxacin for 5 day course. No signs of CHF on exam. ACS unlikely given neg troponins and unchanged EKG. PE unlikely since neg d-dimer and pt has had her baseline normal activity lately with no leg swelling. OSA is very likely contributing to pts respiratory compromise. Pt declines CPAP use. Pt was strongly encouraged to ___ with pulmonologist to address her OSA and asthma. DM2: Difficult control due to steroid administration. Given ISS and glargine 34 U inhouse. HTN: well controlled on home diovan and HCTZ. Microcytic anemia: Pt with MCV ___. HCT ___, at baseline since ___. Anemia panel showed iron deficiency, likely secondary to menses. Recommend outpatient workup including colonoscopy if indicated. Patient will start ferrous sulfate 325mg after finishing levofloxacin (note binding may decrease absorption of levofloxacin). Thrombocythemia: Plt in the 500s, likely from acute infectious/inflammatory state or iron deficiency. Pt has had thrombocythemia in ___ and ___. Leukocytosis: WBC 11->9. Likely from steroids vs potential underlying pneumonia. She was continued on levofloxacin for 5 day course. Pending: Transition of care: -Likely needs OSA and asthma workup with outpatient pulmonologist -FULL code -Encourage continued smoking cessation -iron def anemia, should get outpatient workup
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath. Your symptoms are likely multifactorial: asthma flair, obstructive sleep apnea that is not controlled, and pneumonia. You were given neb treatments, antibiotics, steroids, and your symptoms improved. It is very important to follow up with the sleep specialists to use a machine when you sleep. If you do not do this, you risk getting very serious lung problem called pulmonary hypertension, you also risk have heart failure. These are serious conditions that can likely be avoided if you use the CPAP machine. Please STOP smoking. Smoking will continue to worsen your lung function and will likely cause more significant breathing difficulty and lung damage in the future. Please talk to your primary care if you decide you need a nicotine patch or another quitting aid. Please DO NOT smoke while using oxygen as this is a severe fire hazard.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y.o. man with DM2, ESRD on HD, ___, presents with N/V/D, fever, and coffe ground emesis, new cardiomyopathy. . CARDIOMYOPATHY: Patient with progressive dyspnea over the past year and interval decline in EF. He had a history of exposure to a ___ endemic country and was found to be Chagas IgG positive, IgM negative, concerning for Chagas cardiomyopathy. However, the pattern of heart disease was somewhat inconsistant, and he may have other causes for cardiomyopathy. Of note, his Ferritin was > ___ and his Fe/TIBC ratio was 65%, suggestive of hemachromatosis. However, genotyping showed him to be negative for the common mutations in this disorder. Further differential includes ischemic, ideopathic, infectious, or ESRD-associated. No family history or history of toxin exposure. ESR 46, ___ 1:40, TSH 3, HIV negative. Patient was continued on maximum dose ___ and his beta blocker was uptitrated slightly. Torsemide was restarted. - Would recommend initiation of antitrypanosomal treatment. These results came back after discharge and were communicated to patient's primary care physician. - Outpatient cardiology follow up scheduled - Continue carful volume control at HD - Would consider cardiac MRI and stress MIBI to differentiate etiologies of cardiomyopathy. . UPPER GI BLEED: Coffee ground emesis most likely from upper GI source. Most likely ___ tear vs. PUD. EGD was deferred in the setting of hypotension. Patient refused transfusion in ICU, and Hct improved without intervention. Hematemesis resolved. - Continue on BID PPI for one month. . FEVERS, NAUSEA, VOMITING, DIARRHEA: Patient presented with somewhat atypical flu symptoms of unclear duration. He was found to be Influenza positive. He was initially treated with antibiotics (Vanc/levo), but these were stopped. He was discharged on droplet precautions to end 8 days after symptom onset. . WHEEZING: ___ be cardiac asthma vs. undiagnosed mild intermittant asthma. He was started on albuterol. - Reccomend outpatient PFTs . END-STAGE RENAL DISEASE: Pt receives HD on MWF, and was contunued on a regular schedule. . ANEMIA : B/L Hct in low to mid ___. Serial Hct stabilized. Patient refused blood transfusion . DIABETES: Insulin dependent. BG > 300 upon admission. Per OMR takes 45 units of 70/30 in AM and 40 in ___, but per patient he only takes ___ units. He was started on 20 units NPH BID with plan to give ___ dose while NPO. He was discharged on 20 units of 70/30 twice daily. - continue to monitor and titrate blood sugars PRN . HYPERTENSION: On BB, ___. . HYPERLIPIDEMIA: pravastatin/tricor.
summary: You were admitted for fevers, muscle aches, and chills after hemodialysis. These symptoms were caused by the influenza virus. It will take seven days for this virus to run its course. In the meantime, you should take tylenol as needed for aches and pains. You should wear a mask for seven days from symptom onset to prevent transmission to others. We also did studies that showed your heart to be functioning poorly. This condition is called cardiomyopathy or heart failure. Given this condition, you should be careful to avoid excess salt in your diet and continue to weigh yourself daily. You should continue your Avapro and metoprolol to treat this condition. We initiated some tests to look for treatable causes of your cardiomyopathy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRESENTATION: Ms. ___ is an ___ yo F with h/o CAD, HTN, DM2, DVT on coumadin, and asthma (Dx ___ years ago, with restrictive & obstructive features), presenting with wheezing, cough, and SOB. She was found to be hypoxic, CXR was negative, and she was admitted for management of an asthma exacerbation in the setting of URI. She improved on nebs and steroids with no residual wheezing or SOB, however remained in the hospital for hyperglycemia to 400s-500s. ACUTE: 1. SOB / ASTHMA EXACERBATION: Managed as an acute asthma exacerbation in the setting of an URI. Other possibilities considered in the DDx were pneumonia (ruled out with clear CXR, no leukocytosis or fever), vs. PE (less likely as SOB was with subacute onset, Pt had been therapeutic on her coumadin, Wells risk 1-3%), vs. CHF (less likely as Pt not volume overloaded, cardiac exam benign), vs. hematological (anemia and other abnormalities ruled out with CBC). On admission, she received albuterol and ipratropium nebs, IV solumedrol and her inh steriod was transitioned to fluticasone-salmeterol. Her SOB and wheezing resolved gradually residual resolved. Peak flow which was 160 cc/sec during the exacerbation, improved to best of 260 cc/sec (ideal for age/height: 320) the next day. After receiving IV and PO steroids she was found to be hyperglycemic (see below); steroids were changed to INH form prior to discharge. Of note, her asthma history is somewhat atypical, as diagnosed for the first time at older age; PFTs on record show combined obstructive and restrictive disease features. Pt reported she did not have an albuterol inhaler at home, though it was in her meds list. As her asthma is estimated to be moderate-persistent (wakes up multiple times per month with SOB/wheezing), she would benefit from continued management. At time of discharge; oxygen saturation was ___ on RA at rest and with ambulation. Peak flow ~260. OUTPATIENT ISSUES: [] Continue albuterol nebs Q4H-6H PRN for wheezing [] Continue fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 INH BID (transitioned from budesnide to long acting beta-agonist plus steroid inhouse) [] Continue additional fluticasone propionate 110 mcg INH BID x 1 week (with instruction to rinse mouth after use) [] Patient education on inhaler use done, materials given [] f/u chest imaging - pcp made aware [] Patient will follow up with PCP, ___. 2. HYPERGLYCEMIA: At baseline she has sub-optimally controlled Type 2 diabetes, on glyburide only with daily fasting FSGs in 200s-250s and last HbA1c 8.5% in ___. Glyburide was held at admission, and the Pt was managed on SS insulin. Patient experienced hyperglyceremia to 500s in setting of systemic steroids. ___ team was consulted and involved in optimizing her glucose management with decision made to discharge on basal insulin. Her Lantus dose was simplified, sliding scale was deferred as deemed to complex and potentially danger as outpatient. Additionally metformin was started; ___ 24hr prior to discharge ranged: 150s-280s. Insulin teaching completed. Prescriptions were written for new home glucometer, lancets and strips prior to discharge. OUTPATIENT ISSUES: [] Continue Lantus 12 units SQ QHS; titrate as needed [] Continue Metformin 500 PO BID, uptitrate as needed [] Patient will follow up with PCP, also will receive VNS visits CHRONIC: 3. DVT on COUMADIN: Therapeutic during admission and continuing home dosing; INR was therapeutic except was 3.1 on ___, normalized to 2.2 the next day. [] Continue warfarin schedule: 5 mg PO on ___, 2.5 mg PO on ___ 4. INACTIVE: [] HTN: Continue atenolol. [] HLD: Continue simvastatin and niacin. [] POST NASAL DRIP: - Started Fexofenadine 60 mg PO BID. [] Bladder spasm: continue oxybutinin Please see medications list and appointments attached.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to shortness of breath which was thought to be an asthma flare. You were treated with inhalers as well as steroids to control the inflammation. The steroids improved your breathing but caused your blood sugars to be elevated and you were started on insulin. You will continue to take insulin as an outpatient with close monitoring of your sugars at home. Prior to discharge your oral steroids were discontinued and we opted to start you on flovent (an inhaled steroid) for 7days to help your breathing. The Flovent does not effect your sugars however you must rinse your mouth after use. Please see the changes to your medications below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y.o. male w/ OSA on CPAP, HTN, Asthma p/w fevers, SOB, hemoptysis, transferred to ICU for hypoxia, lobar consolidation, now being transferred to medical floor. . ##. Hypoxia: likely from CAP in setting of obesity hypoventilation syndrome and reactive airway disease/bronchospasm given physical exam and imaging. CXR showing, "opacity in the left retrocardiac region obscuring the left hemidiaphragm, could represent atelectasis or early infection." These factors likely led to shunt, A-a gradient, and hypoxia. CTA chest without PE. Initially transfererd to MICU, requiring shovel mask 10L and continuous nebulizers, subsequently weaned to ___ oxygen by NC in the MICU, then over the course of two days, oxygenating well with minimal oxygen support. Patient was started on levofloxacin for CAP on admission, and will complete an 8 day regimen on discharge. He was also continued on albuterol nebulizers as needed. He was provided incentive spirometry, which he will continue on discharge. Patient continued CPAP at night, per home settings. He was > 92% on RA when ambulatory, prior to discharge. . ##. Hemoptysis: small volume (1 teaspoon) hemoptyis, which had resolved by discharge. Hematocrit and upper airway were stable throughout hospitalization. Suspect source to be from pulmonary circulation/pneumonia. CTA neg for PE or tumor burden. PPD was negative. Patient was started on scheduled benzonatate, which he can take as needed on discharge. . ##. Acute Kidney Injury: resolved. Pt's creatinine on admission elevated at 1.5 but trended down and 1.1 on discharge. Suspect pre-renal causes due to increased insensible losses from fever and poor PO intake during shortness of breath/pneumonia. . ##. HTN: continued on home regimen of Clonidine. Blood pressures were slightly elevated on this admission (consistently recorded as 160s/100s, manual 150s/90s). This was communicated with the patient and PCP. He is to discuss the possibility of adding a new blood pressure agent with his PCP at the ___ visit. . ##. Microcytic anemia: patient with mild anemia, without evidence of bleeding to explain degree of anemia. Work-up with iron studies suggested a component of anemia of chronic disease as well as iron deficiency. Results and implications were communicated with patient and primary care doctor. Patient may require iron supplementation, early colonoscopy, and/or hemoglobinopathy work-up. . ##. PSYCH: pt with h.o. bipolar d.o., depression. Continued on home regimen of Citalopram and Buproprion. Home trazodone was resumed when hypoxia improved. . ##. OSA: continued on home CPAP regimen
summary: You were admitted to ___ for shortness of breath and fevers, which was felt to be from pneumonia. You also had small volume of coughing up blood, which was felt to be related to the underlying pneumonia. You underwent CT scan of the chest which showed no blood clot in the lungs (no pulmonary embolus). Your coughing up blood was resolved for 24 hours prior to discharge. In addition, you did not require supplemental oxygen on discharge, and your oxygen levels were appropriate, even when ambulatory. You will complete a course of levofloxacin antibiotic for pneumonia, as noted below. In addition, please use the incentive spirometry on discharge (10x/hour). Finally, your blood counts were slightly low during admission. Your iron studies were checked, and you may need to start iron supplementation after discussion with your primary care doctor. Please discuss your low blood counts (anemia) with your primary care doctor. You may require additional blood work as well as early colonoscopy for this. Lastly, your blood pressure was a bit on the high side during your admission. We would like you to discuss this with your primary care doctor as well to decrease your risk of heart disease.

summary: You were admitted to the hospital for sore throat, cough, congestion shortness of breath, wheezing, and ear pain. You were started on antibiotics (levofloxacin) to treat for a possible ear infection or sinus infection. You had a chest x-ray that did not show pneumonia. Your shortness of breath and wheezing improved with nebulizer treatments. You should continue taking levofloxacin as prescribed (through ___. You should discuss your asthma with your primary care doctor and consider starting daily controller medications so that you do not have frequent flares. text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o female former ___ from ___ with PMH of rheumatic heart disease, fungal/NTM pulmonary infection who presented to her PCP with cough, SOB, chest pain, found to have cavitary lung lesions on CT and a positive AFB. # Cavitary lung consolidations: Pt has a history of lung consolidations dating from ___ for which she was treated for presumed TB x 3, 9 months each, with 4 drugs thus she was likely treated with RIPE. A bronchoscopy in ___ was negative for MTB PCR, positive for non-tubercular mycobacterium and had moderate growth of trichosporon fungus for which she was treated with anti-fungal and anti-bacterial medication. Since ___ she has experienced persistent cough, wheezing and fatigue not responsive to albuterol/azithromycin. The week prior to admission, patient developed chest pain with SOB and was found to have cavitary lung lesions on CT w/ 2x AFB positive, a positive quantiferon gold and a negative NAAT. Positive quantiferon gold likely represents prior TB infection. Positive acid fast smear x2 in addition to negative NAAT x1 and positive cavitary lesions on CT represent a likely NTM infection. To better rule out tuberculosis would require second negative NAAT which was pending upon discharge. Of note, likely non-tuberculosis mycoplasma (NTM) infection does not rule out other fungal infections or separate pulmonary pathologies that could have predisposed pt to NTM. # Chest pain # Exertional dyspnea: Patient reported pleuritic chest pain, worse with deep breathing and cough, and dyspnea on exertion. She denied orthopnea and PND. H/o rheumatic heart disease however cardiac exam was normal. Likely pain was d/t lung infection and inflammation of the pleura. Unlikely due to cardiac etiology such as MI or PE given EKG shows sinus rhythm, regular rate, normal axis, without ST changes, TTE is notable for moderate TR and mild pulmonary artery hypertension. . # Asthma: patient reports wheezing that started during her prophylactic treatment for TB in ___, discussed above. She has never been diagnosed with asthma, but reports wheezing that responds to albuterol inhaler. She uses her inhaler about 2x a week, no nighttime needs. Unclear if pt has asthma or whether wheezing is ___ infectious lung processes discussed above TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Pt should NOT go to work and should remain at home unless absolutely necessary until second NAAT returns negative. Pt must wear masks if she goes outside her house and especially if she goes to the hospital. Immunocompromised patients and children less than ___ yo should avoid contact with her. [] F/u with ID [] F/u sputum cultures [] F/u NAAT x2 [] Likely outpatient bronchoscopy will be required [] Please evaluate diagnosis of asthma after infectious work up has been completed [] Echo was normal, EKG reassuring, but consider stress test if ongoing dyspnea on exertion and chest pain # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: ___ (Husband) - ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having coughing and your phlegm showed that you could be growing tuberculosis. Based on your preliminary sputum sample it is unlikely that you are growing tuberculosis, but we need to wait for the data from the second culture to come back before we can officially say that you do not have tuberculosis. You do likely have a different bacterial and possibly fungal infection in your lung that will require treatment with antibiotics with or without antifungal treatment. This will need to be investigated further and you likely will require a bronchoscopy (a procedure where they put an instrument in your lungs to collect a sample from your lungs) in clinic after you leave the hospital. For now while you have not been officially ruled out for tuberculosis you should NOT go to work and you should avoid leaving your house unless necessary. If you do leave your house and especially if you go to the clinic or hospital you MUST wear a face mask. People who are immunocompromised or children under ___ should NOT come in contact with you (i.e. they should not be in your house). The final results should be back by ___ and the infectious disease doctor ___ contact you with the results.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ female history of idiopathic tracheal stenosis status post resection and reconstruction c/b restenosis requiring stenting presents with 4 days of increasing dyspnea and increased sputum production. # Dyspnea: Ms. ___ presented to ___ after experiencing four day history of increased dyspnea associated with increased sputum production. Given her history of tracheal stent there was concern regarding the patency of the stent. A CT scan of the trachea without contrast with 3-D reconstruction revealed a patent tracheal stent that remained in place without migration, stent narrowing or evidence of tracheal perforation. There was evidence of a small amount of secretions in the stent that did not significantly narrow the lumen. The thought was that the increased dyspnea was related to increased sputum production and intermittent narrowing of the the tracheal lumen stent. CXR did not reveal any source of infection. Interventional pulmonary was consulted who recommended a bronchoscopy to rule out any obstruction of the stent. She was given one dose of vancomycin/piperacillin tazobactam as well as mucinex which helped clear the mucus. Her breathing improved after mucus production decreased. She underwent a bronchoscopy which revealed a patent tracheal stent. Her previous stent was replaced with a new stent. Given mucus as well as a biofilm on the previous stent, Interventional Pulmonary recommended discharge on augmentin for a total course of 10 days. She was discharged on this medication regimen. At the time of discharge she was saturating 100% on RA and breathing comfortably. She is set up for a follow with Interventional Pulmonary with Dr. ___ ___ on ___. # ASTHMA: Ms. ___ has history of asthma. On presentation she did have wheezing on physical examination. She was continued on her home medications of albuterol, guaifenesin, hydroxyzine, and symbicort. As noted above, she was saturating well on room air. When mucus production decreased her wheezing decreased as well. She was breathing comfortably at the time of discharge. # DEPRESSION: History of depression. She was continued on her home dose of sertraline while inpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== #MEDICATION REGIMEN: Please discuss medication regimen as she is currently taking two inhaled steroids (beclomethasone diopropionate, as well as budesonide-formoterol). #CT TRACHEA WITHOT CONTRAST WITH 3D INCIDENTALOMA: New 2 mm nodule in the lateral basal segment of the right lower lobe does not require further surveillance in the absence of high risk factors. Also notable for hepatic steatosis #ANTIBIOTIC REGIMEN: She was discharged on Augmentin 875 mg PO Q12 for total antibiotic course of 10 days with end date on ___. #FOLLOW UP TESTS: ___: Fluid, other gram stain, respiratory culture; acid fast smear; acid fast culture; fungal culture; potassium hydroxide preparation, ___: blood cultures x2 # CODE: Full Code (confirmed) # CONTACT: HCP ___ (father): ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ after experiencing shortness of breath in the setting of mucus production. This was concerning for mucus plugging of your tracheal stent. You underwent a CT scan of the neck which showed mild mucus plugging of the stent. You received antibiotics (vancomycin, piperacillin tazobactam) for one day. You also received a medication called mucinex to help clear up the congestion. These helped improve your symptoms and decreased the mucus production. You also underwent a procedure called a bronchoscopy to assess the stent. The bronchoscopy did reveal mucus. Your tracheal stent was replaced. Due to the mucus that was noted, the lung doctors would ___ to take an antibiotic called augmentin. Please take this medication (875 milligrams, 125 milligrams) EVERY 12 HOURS with end date on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y.o. Male with CLL, COPD, Asthma, DM II who was apparently referred to the ED for a mass in his lungs found on CT scan and recent right sided neck pain. . #)Incidentaloma: Patient denied any neck pain in the hospital and had a full ROM with no eivdence of LAD or trauma. Per the patient, he was sent here because his PCP told him to come in due to a lung mass seen on a CT chest. Patient's record from at___ were retrieved. The chest CT report noted a new RUL 10-12mm lesion. His case was discussed with his oncologist and PCP and it was agreed that this case was better followed up as an outpatient. He is scheduled for an MRI on ___ and will follow up wth both his PCP and oncologist within ___ days. . #) COPD: While in the hospital, patient had audible weezing with no episodes of word dyspnea or tripoding. He denied any change in his breathing pattern/effort. His CXR portable shows reticular interstitial pattern. There was no evidence of consolidation. Patient reported no worsening of his cough or SOB. He has noted no recent change in his sputum frequency or quality. WBC count was 25.8 (50% lymphocytes) which was attributed to a combination of CLL and his steroid use. Patient's BNP was 337. Due to patient's wheezing and history of COPD, he was treated for a COPD exacerbation. He was started on prednisone and albuterol/ipratroprium nebs and azithromycin in the ED. Due to an absence of sputum production his Azithromycin was stopped. He did have an O2 requirement in the high 80's off of O2 which dropped further on ambulation. He was discharged home with instructions to ___ his O2 at all times especially with ambulation. He was dichared home on advair, spiriva and fluticasone inhalers and advised. He is scheduled to follow up with his PCP in the next ___ days. . #) Hyperkalemia. Patient's potassium was noted to be elevated up to 5.7 on admission with no EKG changes. He was treated with 30 g Kayexalate. His potassium was 5.0 at discharge. . #) DMII: Oral meds were held on admission. His sugars were well controlled on SSI. He was dischargde home on his home meds. . #) Leukocytosis: Noted to be 31.5 on admission and believed to be secondary to CLL and steroids. He dropped to 26 by the morning following admissionand this was noted to be baseline compared to atrius records.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a mild exacerbation of COPD. It is unlikely that you have bacterial pneumonia. We reviewed the report from your CT scan which showed a 1.5 cm density that could be inflammation or malignancy. It was recommended that you have a repeat CT scan in months, in addition to any other evaluation recommended by your outpatient providers. You were started on a new inhaler called Spiriva (tiotropium). Your prednisone taper was changed slightly. Please continue using oxygen as previously prescribed. Please talk with your primary care doctor and with a pulmonology specialist, if you so desire, about increasing your use of supplemental oxygen.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, hypertension, myocardial bridging, diastolic congestive heart failure, asthma who presented with worsening chest pain. Anihypertensive home medications were initially held but a NTG drip was started. Chest pain alleviated with the NTG. EKG stable with prior studies. Trop negative. Patient became hypotensive on the NTG gtt and Cr bumped to 2.3 from 1.0. NTG stopped immediately and patient was given fluids with good response. Given preload dependence of HCM, decision was made to hold all home medications including Diltiazem, HCTZ, Imdur, Lisinopril. Metoprolol was started at a low dose and patient tolerated it well. Echo showed: 1) Severe asymmetric left ventricular hypertrophy suggestive of non-obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. No resting LVOT gradient. 2) Normal biventricular regional/global systolic function. 3) Grade III diastolic dysfunction with mild to moderate type II pulmonary hypertension in setting of normal RV size/function and RA size. 4) Moderate mitral regurgitation in the absence of valvular/chordal ___. Given findings, disopyramide was deferred after discussion with patients cardiologist. On discharge, pertinent cardiac medications include Metoprolol tartate 6.25 q6H. Of note, her course was complicated by right flank pain. UA with negative nitrites and small leukocyte esterase, small blood. Patient remained afebrile. Renal ultrasound was done to evaluate for stones (given her long standing history of nephrolithiasis)which showed no hydropnephorsis, stones or hydroureter. Patient had resolve of CVAT <24 hours. Decision for further imaging or antibiotics deferred as symptoms resolved. # Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Likely etiology of CP. Discontinued on all home medications and cautiously added metoprolol. Patient tolerated it well and went back to her baseline chest pain. Repeat TTE on ___ with no evidence of resting LVOT gradient. Remains CP with activity. Chronic issues: # HTN: Discontinued diltiazem and lisinopril. Continued metoprolol. as above # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine # Asthma: Continued Advair, albuterol nebs prn # Depression: Continued sertraline # Non-insulin dependent DM: Discharged on home medication of metformin. Transitional issues: 1. Patient requires a CPAP machine at home 2. Follow up with cardiology for further management of on going chest pain at rest 3. Follow up with primary care for flank pain, consider urological evaluation as patient has had several stones in the past 4. AVOID diuretics and nitrates as much as possible given pre-load dependence.
