Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb 1 NEB NEB Q6H:PRN shortness of breath 7. Nitroglycerin SL 0.3 mg SL Q5MIN:PRN chest pain 8. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q4H:PRN SOB 9. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID:PRN Constipation 10. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q2H:PRN SOB, wheezing 11. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (500/50) 1 INH IH BID 12. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 13. MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 500 mg PO BID 14. Sertraline 50 mg PO DAILY 15. Lisinopril 20 mg PO BID
summary: You were admitted because of chest pain and shortness of breath. We did an EKG to evaluate your heart function. The EKG was similar to your prior EKGs which was reassuring. We also measured a troponin level, this is something that is found in the blood when you heart is in danger. Your troponin level was normal which was also reassuring. We gave you a medication called nitroglycerin to decrease your chest pain. Although this did alleviate your pain, it caused your blood pressures to become low which caused some injury to your kidneys. We immediately discontinued that medication and gave you fluids. Your kidneys recovered nicely. As you know, you have a condition called hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Your heart muscle is very thick, so much so that it cannot hold as much blood as it normally should. You were on several medications prior to your admission that can cause the amount of blood in your heart to decrease even more.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with history of difficult to control asthma with historical intubation for asthma who presents with a few days of worsening DOE and wheezing in the setting of cold exposure but no evidence of URI or respiratory infection. Active Diagnoses ================ # Moderate Persistent Asthma with exacerbation: Based on her history of asthma and her symptoms of dyspnea, wheezing, cough, and chest tightness this was initially felt to be consistent with an asthma flare. She was on medium dose fluticasone, albuterol, and montelukast prior to admission. She states that she had palpitations on Advair previously, presumably from the salmeterol. She had no significant improvement on standing albuterol/ipratropium nebs and steroids in the ER for 24 hours so she was admitted. She again failed to improve after 48 hours of inpatient continuation of steroids/nebs. CXR was not concerning for PNA and she had no leukocytosis. Pulmonology was consulted who felt the time course for her symptoms was not classic for an asthma exacerbation. They recommended CT trache to evaluate for tracheobronchomalacia. However, before this was completed the patient's symptoms rapidly improved so that further testing was deferred to the outpatient setting. Of note, her peak flows were 430-450 at the peak of her symptoms and she was not hypoxemic, though she was tachypneic to the high ___. She was treated with a steroid burst for 6 days. She was discharged on Advair (fluticasone-salmeterol) instead of fluticasone inhaler so that she would have a ___. If she does not tolerate the salmeterol because of palpitations/tachycardia, she may benefit from higher dose fluticasone (440mcg BID). #Lactatemia: Patient had a lactate on ABG of 5.8 on ___. This was thought to be due to a combination of metformin (continued on admission) and repeated albuterol nebulizers, which may cause elevated lactate in the absence of tissue hypoperfusion. Chronic Diagnoses ================= #Diabetes mellitus type 2 Documented history of diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and insulin at home. While an inpatient, her metformin was initially continued. She had poorly controlled glucose while on steroids and was told to take 40 units of Lantus for the 2 days post-discharge while on steroids and then to resume her home regimen. #Hypertension Documented history of hypertension treated with losartan, hydrochlorothiazide, clonidine, and nifedipine. Her home regime was increased slightly while an inpatient, but she was discharged on her home medications. Transitional Issues =================== -consider outpatient evaluation of her symptoms to exclude tracheobronchomalacia or subglottal stenosis -consider increasing steroid dose, especially if the patient does not tolerate salmeterol -consider outpatient PFTs
summary: you were admitted after your shortness of breath and wheezing did not improve with nebulizers and steroids in the ER. While you were an inpatient you were treated with steroids and nebulizer treatments for your wheezing. We initially thought that your symptoms were from an asthma flare because you did not have signs of an infection. Typically asthma gets better more rapidly, but you were having symptoms after 3 days of treatment. We had the lung specialists see you and they recommended more advanced imaging of your chest. However, your symptoms resolved suddenly on the day of discharge so we thought that you could have this done as an outpatient. While you were here your blood pressure and blood sugars were a little higher because of the steroids. This is temporary.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ======== This is a ___ female patient ___ s/p C-section on ___ (breech} who was readmitted with 3 days of progressive exertional dyspnea, productive cough, and pleuritic chest pain, as well as progressive ___ edema that began a few days after C-section. On arrival, found to be hypertensive (171/81) and SpO2 88% on RA - escalated to BiPAP in the ED with lower extremity edema on exam, CXR w/ pulmonary edema, and bedside echo showing EF ~40% concerning for pre-eclampsia w/ severe features (SBP > 160, headache, pulm edema). She was given multiple 20mg IV Lasix boluses, a nitro gtt, ibuprofen/acetaminophen, and magnesium infusion and her symptoms improved greatly. Her oxygen requirement was weaned to room air over two days as we continued to get fluid off, and her nitro gtt was weaned with captopril being introduced and titrated as appropriate for afterload reduction. Formal echo showing an EF of 35-40%. Patient transferred to the Ob-Gyn service on ___ ___ on ___. She remained stable and cardiology was consulted on ___ for further medical optimization. She was transitioned from captopril to lisinopril 20mg qday and started on metoprolol 12.5mg qday. Further diuresis was deferred given her euvolemic state. She did remarkably well and was meeting all milestones by ___. She was ambulatory on room air without SOB or desaturations. She was clinically euvolemic and her blood pressures were under control on her current regimen. She was discharged home with close cardiology and Obstetric follow-up in place. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Acute HFrEF exacerbation #Peripartum cardiomyopathy She has no significant hx of cardiac disease including cardiomyopathy. The most likely trigger for her dyspnea and volume overload is peripartum cardiomyopathy given her pregnancy w/ C-section done on ___. Her BP was also elevated on admission concerning for pre-eclampsia, as below. In regards to other etiologies, she has no significant hx of tachyarrhythmias, and given her previous lipid profile and A1c in the setting of her young age, CAD is less likely. Other considerations would include vial prodrome [reports recent sputum production but no sick contacts], coronary dissection [less likely], PE [less likely] and HIV induced cardiomyopathy although she has been negative for HIV in the past. Responded well to intermittent IV Lasix boluses and a nitro gtt for afterload reduction that was then weaned to captopril being titrated as needed. #Delayed onset postpartum pre-eclampsia w/ severe features She presented 17 days after C-section with dyspnea; she meets the criteria based on a systolic blood pressure ___ mmHg, headache, and pulmonary edema. Her urine protein was <4 making her SPOT protein/cr ratio <0.4. She denies new-onset cerebral or visual disturbances, or severe persistent RUQ pain. She does not have thrombocytopenia or elevated liver enzymes (initial AST elevated, but specimen hemolyzed) so there is a low concern for HELP syndrome. Started on magnesium infusion and maintained a goal range of 4.8-8.4. Afterload reduction as above. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Asthma She present with dyspnea and has a previous hx of asthma with exam significant for wheezing. The ___ component of her acute dyspnea is related to her acute HF, but Albuterol available as needed.
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have an elevated blood pressure concerning for a condition called pre-eclampsia. You were also found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You were given medications to lower your blood pressure to a normal range and medications to help protect your heart. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ CAD s/p CABG, T2DM, HTN, presenting with SOB, found to have NSTEMI, and heart failure exacerbation. # NSTEMI: Patient with known CAD s/p CABG, now with SOB and found to have NSTEMI. Cath ___ with patent grafts except for occluded vein graft on R with good collaterals. Treated with ASA 81 (received 325 at ___), Atorvastatin 80 mg, home labetalol as below for blood pressure, and home lisinopril 40mg daily. # Acute on chronic diastolic CHF exacerbation: Patient presents with SOB, BNP elevation and crackles on lung exam. Initially reportedly received IVF at ___, then given diuresis with 40 mg IV lasix ___. She also received IV solumedrol and nebs for what was initially felt to be asthma/COPD. Dry on RHC on ___ after diuresis. Continued home lisinopril, labetalol (see below) and discharged on lasix 20 mg 3x/week on discharge to prevent volume overload. # Cough, rhonchi: Patient with cough and wheezes/rhonchi consistent with bronchitis without fevers or focal finding on exam to suggest pneumonia. Dry on cardiac cath so low suspicion for ongoing CHF. Started albuterol inhaler prn and scheduled for outpatient PCP ___. # HTN: Carvedilol started on admission for afterload reduction however in setting of hypertension overnight, restarted labetalol 200mg BID (home dose). Continued home lisinopril, hydral and amlodipine. # T2DM: Held glimepiride and metformin. HISS. # HL: Stopped simvastatin, started atorvastatin 80 mg daily. Added 20mg lasix every ___ and ___. -Started albuterol for bronchospasm related to likely bronchitis. -Will need repeat labs (Cr, K) in 1 week
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and found to have changes on your EKG showing possible damage to your heart. We did a catherization to look at the vessels of your heart but there were no new areas to stent so we recommend continuing with your medical treatment. We also gave you medication to remove excess fluid in your lungs to help your breathing. You were started on lasix (furosemide) to help prevent fluid in your lungs. We have also started you on aspirin and changed you to atorvastatin from simvastatin. Due to your ongoing cough and lung symptoms, we have started a medication called albuterol (an inhaler) which you can take if you are short of breath. Please return to the hospital if you feel short of breath or have chest pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old gentleman with ESRD on peritoneal dialysis (PD), who presented with diffuse abdominal pain, with Staph aureus peritonitis. . . ACTIVE ISSUES: # Peritonitis: He presented with intense, diffuse abdominal pain, subjective fevers/chills, and cloudy peritoneal fluid concerning for peritonitis. Peritoneal fluid was sampled returning 6400 WBC with 96%PMN and culture from the broth eventually grew MRSA. He was initially treated with broad spectrum antibiotics, which were then narrowed to only intraperitoneal vancomycin 1gram dosed by vancomycin level (goal vanc level ___. Typical dosing was every other day. Please administer 1gm vancomycin with ___ morning exchange and let dwell for 6 hrs before draining. Then please check vancomycin level on ___ morning BEFORE vancomycin administration in order to re-dose for ___ administration. He will need IP vancomycin for 3 week course (last day ___. Renal and transplant surgery consults advised primary team to keep current PD catheter with plan to remove if he develops recurrent infection. His pain was initially controlled with tylenol and PRN oxycodone but he has not required narcotics for >24 hours at discharge. At discharge he continued w/ mild abdominal pain (___), which was a significant improvement compared to prior, but was afebrile and without peritoneal signs on exam. . . CHRONIC ISSUES: # ESRD on PD: Stable for past ___ years on PD. Renal follwed the patient and assisted with PD orders. He continued calcitriol and Nephrocaps. . # Asthma: Not using inhalers at baseline, but has been intermittently wheezy on exam during hospitalization. We was treated with albuterol/ipratropium nebs q6 prn SOB/wheezing. . # Hypertension: Continued metoprolol tartrate 25 mg PO BID. . @ Hyperlipidemia: Continued simvastatin 20 mg PO daily. . . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Please administer 1gm vancomycin with ___ morning exchange and let dwell for 6 hrs before draining. Then please check vancomycin level on ___ morning BEFORE vancomycin administration in order to re-dose for ___ administration. # Will need IP antibiotics for 3 weeks (last day of antibiotics will be ___. Goal vancomycin trough level ~15. # CODE: full, confirmed # EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (aunt) ___
summary: You were admitted for abdominal pain and found to have an infection in your peritoneum (a portion of the abdomen). You were treated with antibiotics and your pain improved. You will need to continue intraperitoneal antibiotics for three weeks, ending ___, and this will be administered with your peritoneal dialysis. The plan is to administer 1 gram of vancomycin with your ___ morning exchange and let dwell for 6 hrs before draining. Then you will have a vancomycin level on ___ morning BEFORE vancomycin administration in order to re-dose for ___ administration. This information will be included in the discharge summary to the nephrologist taking care of you.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female ESRD on HD, PVD, HTN, chronic abdominal pain, and asthma who smokes who was admitted for dyspnea and improved after treatment for a COPD exacerbation. BRIEF COURSE ACTIVE ISSUES: # DYSPNEA: Likely multifactorial in etiology, due to an exacerbation of her chronic systolic congestive heart failure and a COPD exacerbation. Pt initially presented to ___ with 4 days worsening dysnpea in the setting of cough and rhinorrhea. Upon presentation, she was hypoxic to 87% on RA requiring up to 4L by NC to achieve saturations between 95-100%. CXR demonstrated pulmonary vasculature fullness. BNP was 30360. She was placed on standing nebulizers, and she was dialyzed to her dry weight. On HD2, she was started on prednisone 60mg and a Z-pack for a 5 day course. Her breathing improved steadily, and on the day of discharge, her wheezing and dyspnea had improved. Pt needs formal PFTs. Follow up with pulmonary per appointment below. # NONSPECIFIC EKG CHANGES: Pt's EKG demonstrated new diffuse ST-T wave abnormalities with borderline prolonged QTc interval. Her troponins were at her baseline and remained stable x 3. CK was WNL. She was monitored on telemetry without incident. Repeat EKG on HD2 demonstrated less prominent ST-T wave abnormalitis, and she denied any chest pain throughout her stay. These changes were thought likely secondary to demand ischemia in the setting of slight volume overload. # UTI: Pt remained asymptomatic throughout her hospitalization. Her urine culture was growing pansenitive Klebsiella pneumoniae. She was started on cefpodoxime 200mg x 3 doses on the day of discharge. # ESRD: Pt received hemodialysis on her normal schedule (T, Th, ___ without complication. # DM TYPE 2: Pt's blood sugars remained on the low side of normal (80-100) but increased to 230s-250s after initiating prednisone. She was started on an insulin sliding scale on HD3 to control her blood sugars while in house. She will check her blood sugars at home. # CHRONIC ABDOMINAL PAIN: Stable. Can continue with home pain regimen and bowel regimen as above (fentanyl patch, percocet, colace, senna). EUS biopsy results demonstrated the following: gastric bx - changes consistent with chemical injury; duodenal bx - chronic active duodenitis, indefinite for dysplasia. Pt should follow up with her gastrointerologist. BRIEF COURSE CHRONIC ISSUES: # ANEMIA: Darbapoetin w/ HD. # HTN: Home amlodipine, metoprolol, and valsartan were continued with good control. # Schizoaffective disorder: Stable. Continued home amitriptyline, aripiprazole, citalopram, risperdal. # CODE: FULL. RECOMMENDATIONS AND TRANSITION OF CARE: 1. ADD Prednisone 60mg daily for 2 more days (last dose on ___. 2. ADD azithromycin 250mg daily for 2 more days (last dose on ___. 3. ADD Cefpodoxime 200mg. Take this ___ and ___ (___). 4. Advair 2 puffs twice per day. Albuterol Q4H PRN shortness of breath. 5. DM2 Monitoring: ___ with meals. Call doctor if your sugars > 300 or less than 75. 6. Weights QAM, call MD if weight goes up more than 3 lbs. 7. Follow up with GI specialist regarding EUS biopsy. 8. Follow up with Pulmonary for dyspnea and for formal PFTs. 9. Follow up with PCP after discharge at appointment listed.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath, wheezing, and cough. Your chext x-ray suggested that there was fluid on your lungs, and a blood test confirmed this. Some extra fluid was taken off in dialysis, which helped reduce the fluid in your lungs. Your EKG also showed some nonspecific changes, so we did some blood tests to monitor your heart. These blood tests were normal. Given your history of smoking and your wheezing, we also treated you for an exacerbation of COPD. You were started on a 5 day course of azithromycin and prednisone. Your wheezing and shortness of breath improved. You will continue the azithromycin and prednisone at home as directed below. Please also use your nebulizers as needed for wheezing or shortness of breath. You were also found to have some bacteria growing in your urine. You were treated with an antibiotic called cefpodoxime.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o female with CAD, asthma, COPD/emphysema, HFpEF, DM type II, and hx of who presents with approximately 1 hour of presyncope described as fairly acute onset of lightheadedness and generalized weakness in the setting of moderate exertion (for her) that was associated with dyspnea on exertion and mild/moderate wheezing, found to be volume overloaded likely ___ acute decompensated heart failure # Presyncope # Acute decompensated HFpEF Acute decompensated heart failure as evidenced by volume overload on exam and pulmonary vascular congestion on CXR. Other ddx included exercise-induced arrhythmias, orthostatic hypertension, and acute exacerbation of reactive airways disease in the setting of recent URI, however there were no events on telemetry, EKG unchanged, orthostatics negative. Normal BNP is difficult to interpret in the setting of obesity. Her dyspnea and generalized weakness improved with diuresis. Repeat CXR also showed improvement in extent of pulmonary edema. It is possible that her presyncope was secondary to overexertion in the setting of acute decompensated heart failure. TTE from earlier this year (___) showed a normal LVEF, with no significant valvular dysfunction, but did show LVH and a significant enlarged LA. . She was diuresed to a dry weight of 222 lbs and discharged on Lasix 20 mg daily. She was advised to increase her lasix if her weight went up by more than 3 lbs. She was discharged with ___. # Iatrogenic hyperthyroidism As above, levothyroxine dose was increased by ~30% in ___ after TSH was found to be 20. This admission, TSH is 0.24 and FT4 1.9. She was discharged on levothyroxine dose of 175 mcg but this should be 150 mcg and I emailed this on ___ to the PCP. # Asthma/Emphysema - continued home Advair; she has significant wheezing with ambulation for which I encouraged albuterol MDI or nebulizer use prior to activity. Chronic Problems # CAD - continued on home aspirin, ezetimibe, carvedilol, (not on home statin due to elevated CK and body aches per review of PCP and ___ notes) # HTN - continued home losartan, carvedilol, amiloride, and amlodipine # Hx of CVA w/ residual deficits & limited mobility - She received ___ evaluation and treatment during her hospital stay. # DM type II - she was continued on home lantus & Humalog insulin regimen w/ sliding scale Humalog TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Please follow up thyroid function tests on lower dose of levothyroxine.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you almost fainted while moderately exerting yourself. You have not been lightheaded during this hospitalization. You also had some increased shortness of breath, for which we gave you Lasix (a diuretic) to remove fluid in your lungs as well as treatments for your asthma. Please be sure to use albuterol (either inhaler or nebulizer) prior to walking. A nebulizer machine is being delivered to your house. Please check your weight daily. Your weight on discharge is 222 pounds. If your weight goes up by more than 3 pounds, please discuss increasing your Lasix dose with Dr ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with CHF, asthma, OSA, pericarditis on prednisone, and a prior PE on warfarin presenting with dyspnea and a nonproductive cough, likely due to an asthma exacerbation. She also had ___ with a Cr 1.5 and lactate up to 4 that improved with IV fluids. ============= ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # ASTHMA EXACERBATION: Presented to the ED with dyspnea. Found to have wheezing and rhonchi on pulmonary exam with no focal consolidation seen on CXR. Patient received duonebs, magnesium sulfate, and methylpred. patient started on 60 mg prednisone thereafter. O2 sats remained in the high ___, and dyspnea subsided on arrival to the floor. Of note on last discharge she had been on continuous oxygen. WHen she presented here she was off oxygen. She remained off of oxygen. On ambulation, her O2 sats were in the mid ___. Pt was discharged with salmeterol + fluticasone and predNasonex taper for further asthma management. She has an outpatient pulmonary followup on ___. # SUPRATHERAPEUTIC INR: INR 7.0 on admission. Given Vitamin K and was 3.7 the following morning. Held her home warfarin on admission. Discussed w/ ___ clinic, instructed Pt to take 5mg warfarin at discharge, followed by return to home dose before short followup on ___ with her PCP. Recommended discussion re: NoAC's. # PRERENAL ___: BUN/Cr at ___ (baseline 0.7) on admission, as well as lactate 4. Appeared dry on exam. Received 4L IVF, to improvement of her BUN/Cr (___) and lactate (1) on discharge. Lasix was held throughout hospitalization. Encouraged Pt to drink to satisfy her thirst at home over the next two days, as well as to monitor daily weights. She was instructed not to take her Lasix at discharge until she discussed further with PCP, or if she gained > 3 lbs in a day. ============== CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # CHF: Held Lasix given signs ___ improved with fluids suggesting prerenal azotemia. # PERICARDITIS: Continued prednisone # PE: Held warfarin during admission. Restarted at 5mg, and then home dose, on discharge. =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== - Please consider whether this Pt should continue Lasix and stringent (< 1.5L) fluid restriction as patient presented with lactate 4.0 and Cr 1.5 - Dry weight reportedly around 175 lbs; her discharge weight was 182.5 lbs (82.8kg) but patient appearing dry on exam. - Please consider starting rivaroxaban when INR <3.0 in place of warfarin. - Witholding Lasix on discharge bc of admission with increased Cr and lactate as above - Started on Advair INH BID for better asthma control # CODE: FULL # CONTACT/HCP: ___ (son) ___
summary: You were seen in our hospital because ___ were having trouble breathing, which we believe was due to an asthma exacerbation. We treated your asthma with nebulizers and a higher dose of steroids while ___ were in the Emergency Department. We will send ___ home with an inhaler for your asthma (Advair), as well as a prednisone taper to gradually bring ___ down to the previous dose of prednisone that ___ had been taking. ___ also had some mild kidney damage when ___ came to us, possibly because ___ were very dehydrated. We gave ___ fluids through the IV, which fixed the damage. Going home, ___ can drink as much fluid as ___ want.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of prior allergic reaction characterized by facial swelling who presents with one week of headache and fever and five days of rash, oral sores and lip/tongue/palate swelling. # Facial Swelling: She was initially transferred to the MICU for monitoring given her substantial facial swelling. She was evaluated by ENT who felt that her airway was not compromised and her swelling was mostly in the anterior mouth. Her facial and lip swelling was felt to represent an allergic reaction, possibly to demerol given the timing. She was placed on famotidine and solumedrol initially and her symptoms improved. She was then transitioned to prednisone for planned 5 day course. She was initially unable to eat but her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was tolerating a regular diet # HSV with Erythema Multiforme Major: She presented with a diffuse papular rash as well as some erythematous nodules, in addition to ulcerations in her mouth. She also had a 5 day history of intermittent fevers. This constellation of findings was felt to represent HSV infection with erythema multiforme major rash. HSV viral culture was sent and is pending at the time of discharge. She was treated with acyclovir and followed by dermatology who recommended bactroban to her EM rash as needed. She had a negative test for HIV, negative ___, and non-reactive RPR. She will complete her course of acyclovir after discharge, and she was given a refill in case she has further outbreaks. # Headache: She reported a headache on presentation which had been previously been evaluated with head CT and LP without concerning findings. It was felt to be related to her HSV infection and it improved during this hospitalization. # Asthma: She had mild wheezing at time during this admission and she was continued on her home flovent and albuterol. Transitional Issues: - Should see dermatology in follow-up - Pending tests: Mycoplasma Ab, HSV Ab, HSV/VZV culture
summary: You were admitted with facial swelling, rash, and fevers. It was felt that your facial swelling is due to an allergic reaction, possibly to the pain medication demerol. Please do NOT take this medication again. You should continue to take prednisone and famotidine for 3 more days for this reaction. In addition, you were found to have an infection in your mouth felt to be likely due to the herpes simplex virus. This virus can cause very painful ulcers in your mouth. You also had a rash on your body and feet that is a reaction to this virus. You are being given a prescription for a cream that you can use on your rash on your body and feet if it is painful. You are also being given a prescription for acyclovir to take for 8 more days to treat the infection. You are written for a refill on this prescription if you have another breakout at a later date.
